**Portugal**, officially the **Portuguese Republic**, is a country located on the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") in [Southwestern Europe](/wiki/Southwestern_Europe "Southwestern Europe"). It is a [unitary republic](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") made up by [mainland Portugal](/wiki/Continental_Portugal "Continental Portugal") and two [autonomous regions](/wiki/Autonomous_Regions_of_Portugal "Autonomous Regions of Portugal"), with [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon") as both its [capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") and [largest city](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities_in_Portugal "List of largest cities in Portugal"). It has a [total population](/wiki/Demographics_of_Portugal "Demographics of Portugal") of over 10.7 million. The mainland [borders](/wiki/Portugal-Spain_border "Portugal-Spain border") [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") to the north and east, with [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira") and the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores") in the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). It features [the westernmost point](/wiki/Cabo_da_Roca "Cabo da Roca") in [continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe").

The western Iberian Peninsula has been inhabited since [prehistory](/wiki/Prehistoric_Iberia "Prehistoric Iberia"), with the earliest signs of [settlement](/wiki/Human_settlement "Human settlement") dating to 5500 BC. Portugal was initially established as a [county](/wiki/County_of_Portugal "County of Portugal") of the [Kingdom of León](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Le%C3%B3n "Kingdom of León") in 868, and [formally](/wiki/Manifestis_Probatum "Manifestis Probatum") as a [kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal") in 1179 resulting from the *[Reconquista](/wiki/Portugal_in_the_Reconquista "Portugal in the Reconquista")* against the Muslims. It later became one of the main participants of the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"), made several seminal advancements in [nautical science](/wiki/Portuguese_nautical_science "Portuguese nautical science") and [was among the first European countries](/wiki/Portuguese_maritime_exploration "Portuguese maritime exploration") to explore and discover new territories and sea routes, establishing [settlements, colonies, and trading posts](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"). After it [became a republic](/wiki/5_October_1910_revolution "5 October 1910 revolution") in 1910, Portugal lived under dictatorship [from 1926](/wiki/28_May_1926_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "28 May 1926 coup d'état") until its [overthrow](/wiki/Carnation_Revolution "Carnation Revolution") in 1974 enabling the [full establishment of democracy](/wiki/Constitution_of_Portugal "Constitution of Portugal") in 1976.

It is a [semi-presidential](/wiki/Semi-presidential_system "Semi-presidential system") constitutional unitary republic and [multi-party](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system") [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") with four separate [sovereignty bodies](/wiki/Sovereignty_body_(Portugal) "Sovereignty body (Portugal)"): [president](/wiki/President_of_Portugal "President of Portugal"), [government](/wiki/Government_of_Portugal "Government of Portugal"), parliament, and [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Portugal "Judiciary of Portugal"). It has a [unicameral national legislature](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") known as the [Assembly of the Republic](/wiki/Assembly_of_the_Republic_(Portugal) "Assembly of the Republic (Portugal)"). Portugal has developed a [complex system](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Portugal "Administrative divisions of Portugal") to manage its territory, even though the mainland continues to remain [highly centralized](/wiki/Centralized_government "Centralized government").

A [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country"), Portugal has an [advanced economy](/wiki/IMF_advanced_economy_list "IMF advanced economy list") relying chiefly upon services, industry, and tourism. Shaped by the various civilisations that have crossed its territory, it has developed a [specific culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Portugal "Culture of Portugal") with [a vast influence](/wiki/Evolution_of_the_Portuguese_Empire "Evolution of the Portuguese Empire") that allowed [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") to become the world's [fifth-most spoken native language](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_number_of_native_speakers "List of languages by number of native speakers") with more than 250 million [native speakers](/wiki/Lusophone "Lusophone"). Portugal is a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area"), and [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"), and one of the founding members of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone"), the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), and the [Community of Portuguese Language Countries](/wiki/Community_of_Portuguese_Language_Countries "Community of Portuguese Language Countries").

## Etymology

The word *Portugal* ([[puɾtuˈɣal]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Portugal.wav "File:Portugal.wav")) derives from the combined [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome")-[Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages") place name [Portus Cale](/wiki/Portus_Cale "Portus Cale")[[16]](#cite_note-23)[[17]](#cite_note-24) (present-day's [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation") of [Porto](/wiki/Porto "Porto") and [Vila Nova de Gaia](/wiki/Vila_Nova_de_Gaia "Vila Nova de Gaia")). Porto stems from the Latin for [port](/wiki/Port "Port"), *portus*; *Cale*'s meaning and origin is unclear. The mainstream explanation is an [ethnonym](/wiki/Ethnonym "Ethnonym") derived from the Callaeci, also known as the [Gallaeci](/wiki/Gallaeci "Gallaeci") peoples, who occupied the north-west of the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula").[[18]](#cite_note-25) One theory proposes *Cale* is a derivation of the Celtic word for 'port'.[[19]](#cite_note-26) Another is that *Cala* was a Celtic goddess. Some French scholars believe it may have come from *Portus Gallus*, the port of the Gauls.[[20]](#cite_note-27)

Around 200 BC, the [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") took Iberia from the [Carthaginians](/wiki/Punic_people "Punic people") during the [Second Punic War](/wiki/Second_Punic_War "Second Punic War"). In the process they conquered Cale, renaming it *Portus Cale* ('Port of Cale') and incorporating it into the province of [Gallaecia](/wiki/Gallaecia "Gallaecia"). During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), the region around Portus Cale became known by the [Suebi](/wiki/Suebi "Suebi") and [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") as *Portucale*. The name *Portucale* changed into *Portugale* during the 7th and 8th centuries, and by the 9th century, it was used to refer to the region between the rivers Douro and [Minho](/wiki/Minho_River "Minho River"). By the 11th and 12th centuries, *Portugale*, *Portugallia*, *Portvgallo* or *Portvgalliae* were already referred to as *Portugal*.[[21]](#cite_note-28)[[22]](#cite_note-29)

## History

Main article: [History of Portugal](/wiki/History_of_Portugal "History of Portugal")

### Prehistory

Main article: [Prehistoric Iberia](/wiki/Prehistoric_Iberia "Prehistoric Iberia")

The region has been inhabited by humans since approximately 400,000 years ago.[[h]](#cite_note-31) Later [Neanderthals](/wiki/Neanderthal "Neanderthal") roamed the northern Iberian peninsula and a tooth has been found at Nova da Columbeira cave in [Estremadura](/wiki/Estremadura_Province_(1936%E2%80%9376) "Estremadura Province (1936–76)").[[24]](#cite_note-32) *[Homo sapiens sapiens](/wiki/Homo_sapiens_sapiens "Homo sapiens sapiens")* arrived in Portugal around 35,000 years ago and spread rapidly,[[25]](#cite_note-33) with the earliest signs of [settlement](/wiki/Human_settlement "Human settlement") dating to 5500 BC.[[26]](#cite_note-CIA-34) Pre-Celtic tribes inhabited Portugal. The [Cynetes](/wiki/Cynetes "Cynetes") developed a written language, leaving [stele](/wiki/Stele "Stele"), which are mainly found in the south.[[27]](#cite_note-35) The [Lusitanians](/wiki/Lusitanians "Lusitanians") occupied central, inland regions.
[Celts](/wiki/Celts "Celts") inhabited the north and center of Portugal mainly, leaving lasting traces in the culture and language.[[28]](#cite_note-36)

### Historical overview of Portugal

Main articles: [Lusitania](/wiki/Lusitania "Lusitania"), [Gallaecia](/wiki/Gallaecia "Gallaecia"), and [Hispania](/wiki/Hispania "Hispania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Roman_Temple_Evora_Portugal_02.jpg/250px-Roman_Temple_Evora_Portugal_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roman_Temple_Evora_Portugal_02.jpg)

The [Roman Temple of Évora](/wiki/Roman_Temple_of_%C3%89vora "Roman Temple of Évora"), built in the 1st century

Romans first invaded the Iberian Peninsula in 219 BC. The Carthaginians, Rome's opponent in the [Punic Wars](/wiki/Punic_Wars "Punic Wars"), were expelled from their coastal colonies. During [Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar")'s rule, almost the entire peninsula was annexed to Rome, facing [resistance](/wiki/Lusitanian_Wars "Lusitanian Wars") from local tribes leaded by [Viriathus](/wiki/Viriathus "Viriathus").[[29]](#cite_note-37)

In 409, with the decline of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire"), the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") was occupied by [Germanic tribes](/wiki/Germanic_tribes "Germanic tribes").[[30]](#cite_note-38) Western Iberia initially formed the [Suebian Kingdom](/wiki/Suebian_Kingdom "Suebian Kingdom")[[31]](#cite_note-39) with its capital in [Braga](/wiki/Braga "Braga"). The [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") eventually defeated the [Suebi](/wiki/Suebi "Suebi") and ruled until the early eighth century.
Most of the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") was invaded from the south and became part of [al-Andalus](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus") in 726, following the establishment of the [Umayyad Caliphate](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate").[[32]](#cite_note-40)

After defeating the [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths"), the Umayyad Caliphate started expanding rapidly in the peninsula. Beginning in 726, the land that is now Portugal became part of the Umayyad Caliphate until its collapse in 750. That year the west of the empire gained its independence under [Abd al-Rahman I](/wiki/Abd_al-Rahman_I "Abd al-Rahman I") with the establishment of the [Emirate of Córdoba](/wiki/Emirate_of_C%C3%B3rdoba "Emirate of Córdoba")[[i]](#cite_note-43) lasting until 1031, with its dissolution into 23 [small kingdoms](/wiki/Taifa "Taifa").[[35]](#cite_note-44)[[36]](#cite_note-45)[[37]](#cite_note-46) Most of present-day Portugal fell into the hands of the [Taifa of Badajoz](/wiki/Taifa_of_Badajoz "Taifa of Badajoz"), and in 1022 the [Taifa of Seville](/wiki/Taifa_of_Seville "Taifa of Seville"). Those petty kingdoms were conquered by the [Almoravids](/wiki/Almoravids "Almoravids") in 1086, then by the [Almohads](/wiki/Almohads "Almohads") in 1147.[[38]](#cite_note-47)

Invasions from the North also occurred in this period, with [Viking](/wiki/Viking "Viking") incursions raiding the coast between the 9th and 11th centuries, including Lisbon.[[39]](#cite_note-48)[[40]](#cite_note-49) This resulted in the establishment of small [Norse](/wiki/Norsemen "Norsemen") settlements in the coastline between [Douro](/wiki/Douro "Douro") and [Minho](/wiki/Minho_Province "Minho Province").[[41]](#cite_note-50)[[42]](#cite_note-51)

### *Reconquista*

Main article: [Portugal in the Reconquista](/wiki/Portugal_in_the_Reconquista "Portugal in the Reconquista")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Estatua_de_Vimara_Peres%2C_Oporto%2C_Portugal%2C_2012-05-09%2C_DD_01.JPG/250px-Estatua_de_Vimara_Peres%2C_Oporto%2C_Portugal%2C_2012-05-09%2C_DD_01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Estatua_de_Vimara_Peres,_Oporto,_Portugal,_2012-05-09,_DD_01.JPG)

[Vímara Peres](/wiki/V%C3%ADmara_Peres "Vímara Peres") was the first [Count of Portugal](/wiki/County_of_Portugal "County of Portugal").

The *[Reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista")* was a period when Christians reconquered the Iberian Peninsula from [Moorish](/wiki/Moors "Moors") domination. An Asturian Visigothic noble named [Pelagius of Asturias](/wiki/Pelagius_of_Asturias "Pelagius of Asturias") was elected leader in 718[[43]](#cite_note-52) by many of the ousted [Visigoth](/wiki/Visigoth "Visigoth") nobles. After defeating the Moors in the [Battle of Covadonga](/wiki/Battle_of_Covadonga "Battle of Covadonga") in 722, Pelagius was proclaimed king, thus founding the Christian [Kingdom of Asturias](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Asturias "Kingdom of Asturias") and starting the war of Christian reconquest.[[44]](#cite_note-V._Livermore,_1969_pp._32-33-53)

In 868, [Porto](/wiki/Porto "Porto"), between the rivers Minho and Douro, was reconquered from the Moors by nobleman and knight [Vímara Peres](/wiki/V%C3%ADmara_Peres "Vímara Peres") on the orders of King [Alfonso III of Asturias](/wiki/Alfonso_III_of_Asturias "Alfonso III of Asturias").[[45]](#cite_note-54)[[46]](#cite_note-55)[[47]](#cite_note-56) Finding many towns in the region deserted, he decided to repopulate and rebuild them.[[48]](#cite_note-QuidNovi-57)

After annexing the County of Portugal, [Alfonso III of Asturias](/wiki/Alfonso_III_of_Asturias "Alfonso III of Asturias") knighted Vímara Peres, in 868, as the first Count of Portus Cale. The region became known as *Portucale*, *Portugale*, and *Portugália*.[[48]](#cite_note-QuidNovi-57) With the forced abdication of Alfonso III in 910, the Kingdom of Asturias split into three separate kingdoms; they were reunited in 924 under the crown of [León](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Le%C3%B3n "Kingdom of León").[[49]](#cite_note-58) The first County lasted until 1071, when the [Kingdom of Galicia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia "Kingdom of Galicia") took over the region following a rebellion.[[50]](#cite_note-59) A few years later, in 1096, [Alfonso VI of León](/wiki/Alfonso_VI_of_Le%C3%B3n "Alfonso VI of León") bestowed the County, thus refounding it, to [Henry of Burgundy](/wiki/Henry,_Count_of_Portugal "Henry, Count of Portugal") and married him to his daughter, [Teresa of León](/wiki/Theresa,_Countess_of_Portugal "Theresa, Countess of Portugal").[[51]](#cite_note-60)

### Independence

Main article: [Portugal in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Portugal_in_the_Middle_Ages "Portugal in the Middle Ages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Alfonso_Enriquez_escultura_Guimaraes.JPG/250px-Alfonso_Enriquez_escultura_Guimaraes.JPG)](/wiki/File:Alfonso_Enriquez_escultura_Guimaraes.JPG)

1887 statue of King Afonso I, by [António Soares dos Reis](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Soares_dos_Reis "António Soares dos Reis").[[52]](#cite_note-61)[[53]](#cite_note-62)[[54]](#cite_note-63)[[55]](#cite_note-64)

After the death of Count Henry, in 1112, a dispute erupted, culminating at the [Battle of São Mamede](/wiki/Battle_of_S%C3%A3o_Mamede "Battle of São Mamede"), in 1128, where [Afonso Henriques](/wiki/Afonso_I_of_Portugal "Afonso I of Portugal") defeated the forces of his mother [Countess Teresa](/wiki/Teresa,_Countess_of_Portugal "Teresa, Countess of Portugal") and her lover [Fernão Peres de Trava](/wiki/Fern%C3%A3o_Peres_de_Trava "Fernão Peres de Trava"), establishing himself as sole leader of the [county](/wiki/County_of_Portugal "County of Portugal") achieving *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* independence.[[56]](#cite_note-65) In 1129, the capital was transferred from Guimarães to Coimbra. Afonso's campaigns were successful and in 1139, he obtained a victory in the [Battle of Ourique](/wiki/Battle_of_Ourique "Battle of Ourique"), so was proclaimed [King of Portugal](/wiki/King_of_Portugal "King of Portugal") by his soldiers. Afonso was recognised as king of Portugal in 1143 by King [Alfonso VII of León](/wiki/Alfonso_VII_of_Le%C3%B3n "Alfonso VII of León"), and in 1179 by [Pope Alexander III](/wiki/Pope_Alexander_III "Pope Alexander III"), with the [papal bull](/wiki/Papal_bull "Papal bull") *[Manifestis Probatum](/wiki/Manifestis_Probatum "Manifestis Probatum")*.[[57]](#cite_note-Jenkins_Sofos_2023_p145-66)

Afonso Henriques and his successors, aided by military [monastic orders](/wiki/Christian_monasticism "Christian monasticism"), continued pushing southwards against the Moors [until 1249](/wiki/Siege_of_Faro_(1249) "Siege of Faro (1249)") with the [capture of Algarve](/wiki/Portuguese_conquest_of_the_Algarve "Portuguese conquest of the Algarve") and the expel of the last Moorish settlements.[[58]](#cite_note-67)[[59]](#cite_note-68) With minor readjustments, [Portugal's borders have remained the same](/wiki/Treaty_of_Alca%C3%B1ices "Treaty of Alcañices"), making it one of the oldest established countries in Europe.[[60]](#cite_note-69)

In 1348-49 Portugal, as with the rest of Europe, was devastated by the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death").[[61]](#cite_note-70) In 1373, Portugal, during the reign of [King Ferdinand I](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_Portugal "Ferdinand I of Portugal"), in the midst of the [Fernandine Wars](/wiki/Fernandine_Wars "Fernandine Wars") made an [alliance with England](/wiki/Anglo-Portuguese_Alliance "Anglo-Portuguese Alliance"), which would be reinforced later with the signing of the [Treaty of Windsor](/wiki/Treaty_of_Windsor_(1386) "Treaty of Windsor (1386)"),[[62]](#cite_note-Windsor-71) making it the oldest standing alliance in the world.[[63]](#cite_note-72)

### Age of Discovery

Main article: [Portuguese Empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/PortugueseColonialEmpire02.png/250px-PortugueseColonialEmpire02.png)](/wiki/File:PortugueseColonialEmpire02.png)

Areas that were, at some point, part of the [Portuguese Empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire")

In 1383 [John I of Castile](/wiki/John_I_of_Castile "John I of Castile") and [Beatrice of Portugal](/wiki/Beatrice_of_Portugal "Beatrice of Portugal"), the only surviving legitimate child of [Ferdinand I of Portugal](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_Portugal "Ferdinand I of Portugal"), claimed the throne of Portugal. [John of Aviz](/wiki/John_I_of_Portugal "John I of Portugal") led a revolt against this claim and defeated the Castilians in the [Battle of Aljubarrota](/wiki/Battle_of_Aljubarrota "Battle of Aljubarrota"), with the [House of Aviz](/wiki/House_of_Aviz "House of Aviz") becoming the ruling house which led Portugal to the limelight of European politics and culture.[[64]](#cite_note-73)[[65]](#cite_note-Carvalho_2014-74)[[66]](#cite_note-75)

Portugal spearheaded [European exploration of the world](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery") under the sponsorship of [Prince Henry the Navigator](/wiki/Prince_Henry_the_Navigator "Prince Henry the Navigator"),[[67]](#cite_note-76)[[68]](#cite_note-Casimiro2020-77) and made several seminal advancements in [nautical science](/wiki/Portuguese_nautical_science "Portuguese nautical science").[[j]](#cite_note-79) The Portuguese explored the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean"), established trade routes in most of southern Asia, and sent the first direct European maritime trade and diplomatic missions [to China](/wiki/Jorge_%C3%81lvares "Jorge Álvares") and [Japan](/wiki/Nanban_trade "Nanban trade"). In 1415, Portugal acquired its first colonies by conquering [Ceuta](/wiki/Ceuta "Ceuta"). Throughout the 15th century, [Portuguese explorers](/wiki/European_exploration_of_Africa#Portuguese "European exploration of Africa") sailed the coast of Africa, establishing trading posts [for commodities](/wiki/Economic_history_of_Portugal#Expansion_of_the_Portuguese_empire_(15th_and_16th_centuries) "Economic history of Portugal"), ranging from [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold") to [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery").[[70]](#cite_note-80)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Portuguese_Carracks_off_a_Rocky_Coast.jpg/250px-Portuguese_Carracks_off_a_Rocky_Coast.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portuguese_Carracks_off_a_Rocky_Coast.jpg)

Portuguese vessels of the Discovery Age depicted in the 16th century painting *Portuguese Carracks Off a Rocky Coast*.[[71]](#cite_note-81)[[72]](#cite_note-82)[[73]](#cite_note-83)

The [Treaty of Tordesillas](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tordesillas "Treaty of Tordesillas"), signed in 1494, divided the newly located lands outside Europe between Portugal and Spain along a line west of [Cape Verde](/wiki/Geography_of_Cape_Verde "Geography of Cape Verde"). In 1500, [Gaspar Corte-Real](/wiki/Gaspar_Corte-Real "Gaspar Corte-Real") reached modern-day [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") and founded the town of [Portugal Cove-St. Philip's](/wiki/Portugal_Cove-St._Philip%27s "Portugal Cove-St. Philip's").[[74]](#cite_note-84)[[75]](#cite_note-85)[[76]](#cite_note-86)
In 1500, [Pedro Álvares Cabral landed on Brazil](/wiki/Discovery_of_Brazil "Discovery of Brazil") and claimed it for Portugal.[[77]](#cite_note-87) Portuguese sailors set out to reach Eastern Asia, landing in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), Japan, [Timor](/wiki/Timor "Timor"), [Flores](/wiki/Flores "Flores"), and the [Moluccas](/wiki/Moluccas "Moluccas").[[k]](#cite_note-93)

Between 1519 and 1522 [Ferdinand Magellan](/wiki/Ferdinand_Magellan "Ferdinand Magellan") organised a Spanish expedition to the [East Indies](/wiki/East_Indies "East Indies") which resulted in the first [circumnavigation](/wiki/Circumnavigation "Circumnavigation") of the globe. The [Treaty of Zaragoza](/wiki/Treaty_of_Zaragoza "Treaty of Zaragoza"), signed in 1529, divided the Pacific Ocean between Spain and Portugal.[[83]](#cite_note-94)

### Iberian Union and Restoration

Main article: [Iberian Union](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union")

A [succession crisis](/wiki/Portuguese_succession_crisis_of_1580 "Portuguese succession crisis of 1580") followed the deaths of [King Sebastian](/wiki/Sebastian,_King_of_Portugal "Sebastian, King of Portugal") and his grand-uncle [King Henry](/wiki/Henry,_King_of_Portugal "Henry, King of Portugal"), both without direct male heirs. [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") claimed the throne in 1580, under the pretext that his mother, [Isabella of Portugal](/wiki/Isabella_of_Portugal "Isabella of Portugal"), was a Portuguese princess. Philip II of Spain was crowned king of Portugal,[[84]](#cite_note-95)[[85]](#cite_note-96) forming a [personal union](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union") of the kingdoms,[[l]](#cite_note-98) depriving Portugal of an independent foreign policy which led to its involvement in the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War").[[87]](#cite_note-99)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Coroa%C3%A7%C3%A3o_de_D._Jo%C3%A3o_IV.png/250px-Coroa%C3%A7%C3%A3o_de_D._Jo%C3%A3o_IV.png)](/wiki/File:Coroa%C3%A7%C3%A3o_de_D._Jo%C3%A3o_IV.png)

A 1650s engraving depicts the accession and coronation of [John IV of Braganza](/wiki/John_IV_of_Portugal "John IV of Portugal") as King of Portugal on 15 December 1640, in Lisbon

From 1595 to 1663 the [Dutch-Portuguese War](/wiki/Dutch-Portuguese_War "Dutch-Portuguese War") involving primarily Dutch companies invading Portuguese [colonies](/wiki/Colonies "Colonies") and commercial interests, resulted in the loss of Portugal's Indian sea trade monopoly.[[88]](#cite_note-100) On 1 December 1640, [John, Duke of Braganza](/wiki/John_IV_of_Portugal "John IV of Portugal") was crowned following an uprising spearheaded by disgruntled nobles and was proclaimed king, ending 60 years of the Iberian Union under the [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") and the beginning of the [House of Braganza](/wiki/House_of_Braganza "House of Braganza"). Following its defeat in the [Portuguese Restoration War](/wiki/Portuguese_Restoration_War "Portuguese Restoration War"), Spain [recognized](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon_(1668) "Treaty of Lisbon (1668)") John as king of Portugal.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnderson2000131-101) The reign of [John V](/wiki/John_V_of_Portugal "John V of Portugal") saw an influx of gold into the royal treasury, supplied largely by the [royal fifth](/wiki/Royal_fifth "Royal fifth") and a [gold rush](/wiki/Gold_rush "Gold rush") that was one of the largest movements from Europe to an American colony.[[m]](#cite_note-103)[[91]](#cite_note-104)

Lisbon was struck by a major [earthquake on 1 November 1755](/wiki/1755_Lisbon_earthquake "1755 Lisbon earthquake").[[92]](#cite_note-105)[[n]](#cite_note-107) Following the earthquake, [King Joseph I](/wiki/Joseph_I_of_Portugal "Joseph I of Portugal") gave his prime minister, [Sebastião José de Carvalho e Melo, 1st Marquis of Pombal](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A3o_Jos%C3%A9_de_Carvalho_e_Melo,_1st_Marquis_of_Pombal "Sebastião José de Carvalho e Melo, 1st Marquis of Pombal"), overarching powers, becoming an [enlightened despot](/wiki/Enlightened_absolutism "Enlightened absolutism").[[94]](#cite_note-108)

### Constitutional monarchy

Main articles: [History of Portugal (1777–1834)](/wiki/History_of_Portugal_(1777%E2%80%931834) "History of Portugal (1777–1834)") and [History of Portugal (1834–1910)](/wiki/History_of_Portugal_(1834%E2%80%931910) "History of Portugal (1834–1910)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Oscar_Pereira_da_Silva_-_Sess%C3%A3o_das_Cortes_De_Lisboa%2C_Acervo_do_Museu_Paulista_da_USP_2.jpg/250px-Oscar_Pereira_da_Silva_-_Sess%C3%A3o_das_Cortes_De_Lisboa%2C_Acervo_do_Museu_Paulista_da_USP_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oscar_Pereira_da_Silva_-_Sess%C3%A3o_das_Cortes_De_Lisboa,_Acervo_do_Museu_Paulista_da_USP_2.jpg)

The [Constituent Cortes of 1820](/wiki/Constituent_Cortes_of_1820 "Constituent Cortes of 1820"), that approved the first constitution of Portugal.

In 1807 Portugal refused [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon")'s demand to join the [Continental System](/wiki/Continental_System "Continental System") of embargo against the United Kingdom; a French invasion under [General Junot](/wiki/Jean-Andoche_Junot "Jean-Andoche Junot") followed, and Lisbon was captured in 1807. British intervention in the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War") helped maintain Portuguese independence; the last French troops were expelled in 1812.[[95]](#cite_note-109) During the [Napoleonic invasions](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), the [Portuguese royal family transferred](/wiki/Transfer_of_the_Portuguese_court_to_Brazil "Transfer of the Portuguese court to Brazil") the court to [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), in Brazil, making it the capital between 1808 and 1821.[[96]](#cite_note-110)

In 1820, a [constitutionalist insurrection](/wiki/Liberal_Revolution_of_1820 "Liberal Revolution of 1820") began in [Porto](/wiki/Porto "Porto") which forced [King John VI](/wiki/John_VI_of_Portugal "John VI of Portugal") and his court to return to mainland Portugal in 1821.[[97]](#cite_note-111)[[98]](#cite_note-112) The death of John in 1826 led to a crisis of royal succession. His eldest son, [Pedro I of Brazil](/wiki/Pedro_I_of_Brazil "Pedro I of Brazil"), briefly became [Pedro IV of Portugal](/wiki/Pedro_IV_of_Portugal "Pedro IV of Portugal"), but neither the Portuguese nor Brazilians wanted a unified monarchy.[[99]](#cite_note-113)

Consequently, Pedro abdicated the Portuguese crown in favour of his 7-year-old daughter, [Maria da Glória](/wiki/Maria_II_of_Portugal "Maria II of Portugal"). Dissatisfaction at Pedro's constitutional reforms led the ["absolutist" faction of landowners and the church](/wiki/Miguelist "Miguelist") to proclaim his brother [Miguel](/wiki/Miguel_I_of_Portugal "Miguel I of Portugal") king in February 1828. This led to the [Liberal Wars](/wiki/Liberal_Wars "Liberal Wars"), in which Pedro forced Miguel to abdicate in favour of his daughter and go into exile in 1834.[[100]](#cite_note-114) Under the constitutional monarchy, the country faced economic crises, political instability, and several coups d'état.[[101]](#cite_note-115) At the same time it expanded its colonies in Africa, but this culminated in the [1890 British Ultimatum](/wiki/1890_British_Ultimatum "1890 British Ultimatum"), which thwarted Portugal's imperialist ambitions and represented a devastating blow to the monarchy.[[102]](#cite_note-116)

### First Republic and *Estado Novo*

Main articles: [First Portuguese Republic](/wiki/First_Portuguese_Republic "First Portuguese Republic") and [Estado Novo (Portugal)](/wiki/Estado_Novo_(Portugal) "Estado Novo (Portugal)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Proclama%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Rep%C3%BAblica_Portuguesa.jpg/250px-Proclama%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Rep%C3%BAblica_Portuguesa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Proclama%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Rep%C3%BAblica_Portuguesa.jpg)

[José Relvas](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Relvas "José Relvas") proclaiming the republic on [5 October 1910](/wiki/5_October_1910_revolution "5 October 1910 revolution"), at [Lisbon City Hall](/wiki/Lisbon_City_Hall "Lisbon City Hall")

In 1908, [King Carlos I](/wiki/Carlos_I_of_Portugal "Carlos I of Portugal") and his son and heir, [Luís Filipe, Prince Royal](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Filipe,_Prince_Royal_of_Portugal "Luís Filipe, Prince Royal of Portugal"), [were assassinated](/wiki/Lisbon_Regicide "Lisbon Regicide") by [republican](/wiki/Portuguese_Republican_Party "Portuguese Republican Party") sympathizers.[[103]](#cite_note-117) Later, in 1910, the monarchy was [replaced](/wiki/5_October_1910_revolution "5 October 1910 revolution") with a republic.[[104]](#cite_note-118) During World War I, Portugal [fought for the Allies](/wiki/Portugal_during_World_War_I "Portugal during World War I"); however, the war hurt its weak economy.[[105]](#cite_note-119)

Political instability and economic weaknesses created chaos and unrest during the [First Republic](/wiki/First_Portuguese_Republic "First Portuguese Republic").[[106]](#cite_note-120) These conditions led to its [overthow](/wiki/28_May_1926_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "28 May 1926 coup d'état") in 1926 and the establishing of the *[Ditadura Nacional](/wiki/Ditadura_Nacional "Ditadura Nacional")*, which evolved into the right-wing dictatorship of the *[Estado Novo](/wiki/Estado_Novo_(Portugal) "Estado Novo (Portugal)")*, under [António de Oliveira Salazar](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_de_Oliveira_Salazar "António de Oliveira Salazar") in 1933.[[107]](#cite_note-121)

Portugal [remained neutral](/wiki/Portugal_during_World_War_II "Portugal during World War II") in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[108]](#cite_note-122) From the 1940s to 1960s, Portugal was a founding member of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), and [EFTA](/wiki/European_Free_Trade_Association "European Free Trade Association"), it also joined the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") in 1955.[[109]](#cite_note-ONU-123)[[110]](#cite_note-EFTA-124) New economic development projects and relocation of mainland Portuguese citizens into the overseas provinces in Africa were initiated, with [Angola](/wiki/Portuguese_Angola "Portuguese Angola") and [Mozambique](/wiki/Portuguese_Mozambique "Portuguese Mozambique") being the main destinations.[[o]](#cite_note-126)[[111]](#cite_note-PTC-125) However, [indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") were [second-class citizens](/wiki/Second-class_citizen "Second-class citizen") and sidelined on those development projects,[[112]](#cite_note-127) which led to the emergence of independence movements in Angola,[[113]](#cite_note-128) Mozambique, and [Guinea](/wiki/Portuguese_Guinea "Portuguese Guinea") in the 1960s, culminating in 1961 in the [Portuguese Colonial War](/wiki/Portuguese_Colonial_War "Portuguese Colonial War"), which lasted until [1974](/wiki/Carnation_Revolution "Carnation Revolution").[[114]](#cite_note-129)

### Return to democracy

Main articles: [Carnation Revolution](/wiki/Carnation_Revolution "Carnation Revolution") and [Third Portuguese Republic](/wiki/Third_Portuguese_Republic "Third Portuguese Republic")

On 25 April 1974, the Carnation Revolution overthrew the *Estado Novo*[[115]](#cite_note-130) beginning the [transition to democracy](/wiki/Portuguese_transition_to_democracy "Portuguese transition to democracy").[[116]](#cite_note-131) Portugal continued to be governed by several provisional governments[[117]](#cite_note-132) until the [legislative election of 1976](/wiki/1976_Portuguese_legislative_election "1976 Portuguese legislative election").[[118]](#cite_note-E1976-133) After the transition to democracy, Portugal flipped between [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") and [neoliberalism](/wiki/Neoliberalism "Neoliberalism").[[119]](#cite_note-134) [Land reform](/wiki/Land_reform "Land reform")[[120]](#cite_note-135) and [nationalisations](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization")[[121]](#cite_note-136) were enforced; the [Portuguese Constitution](/wiki/Portuguese_Constitution "Portuguese Constitution") was rewritten to accommodate socialist and communist principles.[[122]](#cite_note-CR1982-137) most of which were later removed in the constitutional revisions of 1982 and 1989.[[122]](#cite_note-CR1982-137)[[123]](#cite_note-138) In 1986, Portugal joined the European Economic Community, precursor to the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). Portugal's economy progressed considerably as a result of [European Structural and Investment Funds](/wiki/European_Structural_and_Investment_Funds "European Structural and Investment Funds") and easier access to foreign markets.[[124]](#cite_note-CEE-139) Portugal's last overseas territory, [Macau](/wiki/Portuguese_Macau "Portuguese Macau"), was [handed over to China](/wiki/Handover_of_Macau "Handover of Macau") in 1999.[[125]](#cite_note-140) The [2010–2014 Portuguese financial crisis](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932014_Portuguese_financial_crisis "2010–2014 Portuguese financial crisis") led to an international bailout with intense [austerity](/wiki/Austerity "Austerity") policies, resulting in social tensions.[[126]](#cite_note-141)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Portugal](/wiki/Geography_of_Portugal "Geography of Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Portugal_topographic_map-en.svg/250px-Portugal_topographic_map-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Portugal_topographic_map-en.svg)

A [topographic map](/wiki/Topographic_map "Topographic map") of Portugal

Portugal occupies [an area](/wiki/Continental_Portugal "Continental Portugal") on the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") and two archipelagos in the Atlantic Ocean: [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira") and the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores"). It lies between latitudes [30°](/wiki/30th_parallel_north "30th parallel north") and [42° N](/wiki/42nd_parallel_north "42nd parallel north"), and longitudes [32°](/wiki/32nd_meridian_west "32nd meridian west") and [6° W](/wiki/6th_meridian_west "6th meridian west").[[127]](#cite_note-142) The Portuguese territory covers 156,597 km2 (60,462 sq mi).[[128]](#cite_note-143)[[129]](#cite_note-144)[[p]](#cite_note-146)

[Continental Portugal](/wiki/Continental_Portugal "Continental Portugal") is split by the [Tagus](/wiki/Tagus "Tagus"), that flows from Spain and disgorges in the [Tagus Estuary](/wiki/Tagus_Estuary_Natural_Reserve "Tagus Estuary Natural Reserve") at Lisbon, before discharging into the Atlantic. The northern landscape is mountainous towards the interior with several plateaus indented by river valleys, whereas the south[[q]](#cite_note-147) is characterised by rolling plains.[[131]](#cite_note-148)

At an elevation of 2,351 metres (7,713 ft), [Mount Pico](/wiki/Mount_Pico "Mount Pico") in [Pico Island](/wiki/Pico_Island "Pico Island") is the highest peak in the country.[[132]](#cite_note-149)[[r]](#cite_note-151) The archipelagos of Madeira and the Azores are scattered within the Atlantic Ocean: the Azores straddling the [Mid-Atlantic Ridge](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_Ridge "Mid-Atlantic Ridge") on a tectonic triple junction, and Madeira along a range formed by [in-plate](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics") [hotspot](/wiki/Hotspot_(geology) "Hotspot (geology)") geology. These islands were formed by volcanic and seismic events. The [last terrestrial volcanic eruption](/wiki/Capelinhos "Capelinhos") occurred in 1957–58, and minor earthquakes occur sporadically.[[134]](#cite_note-152)

[The exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone_of_Portugal "Exclusive economic zone of Portugal") covers an area of 1,727,408 km2 (666,956 mi2) being the 3rd largest [exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone "Exclusive economic zone") of the European Union and the 20th largest in the world.[[135]](#cite_note-153)

### Climate

Main articles: [Climate of Portugal](/wiki/Climate_of_Portugal "Climate of Portugal") and [Climate change in Portugal](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Portugal "Climate change in Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_PRT_1991%E2%80%932020.svg/1280px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_PRT_1991%E2%80%932020.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_PRT_1991%E2%80%932020.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_PRT_1991%E2%80%932020.svg/960px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_PRT_1991%E2%80%932020.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_PRT_1991%E2%80%932020.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_PRT_1991%E2%80%932020.svg/500px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_PRT_1991%E2%80%932020.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_PRT_1991%E2%80%932020.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_PRT_1991%E2%80%932020.svg/960px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_PRT_1991%E2%80%932020.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_PRT_1991%E2%80%932020.svg)

The [Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of Portugal

The climate of Portugal is mainly [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate").[[136]](#cite_note-154) While the mainland and Madeira show a Mediterranean climate, the Azores mainly has a [temperate oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate"). Despite the size of the country, it has a diverse variety of regional climates.[[137]](#cite_note-Mora_Vieira_The_Climate_of_Portugal-155)

The Iberian Peninsula is located at the southern margin of the temperate zone and at the northern margin of the subtropical [high-pressure zone](/wiki/High-pressure_area "High-pressure area") which is influenced by the seasonal latitudinal
shift of the [jet stream](/wiki/Jet_stream "Jet stream") that directly impacts on the trajectory of the [polar front](/wiki/Polar_front "Polar front"), affecting Portugal's climate. Typically in the winter, the jet stream moves southwards and Portugal becomes under the influence of the polar front, producing colder temperatures. When the polar front moves northward, Portugal becomes under the influence of the stable atmospheric conditions bringing milder weather during the summer.[[137]](#cite_note-Mora_Vieira_The_Climate_of_Portugal-155)

The high altitude zones of the Azorean islands have a [temperate maritime climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") while the western Azores has [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate"). In parts of the [Beja District](/wiki/Beja_District "Beja District") and in [Porto Santo Island](/wiki/Porto_Santo_Island "Porto Santo Island") the climate is [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate"). In the [Selvagens Islands](/wiki/Savage_Islands "Savage Islands") the climate is [hot desert](/wiki/Hot_desert_climate "Hot desert climate"). Portugal is one of the warmest countries in Europe: the average temperature in [mainland Portugal](/wiki/Mainland_Portugal "Mainland Portugal") varies from 10–12 °C (50.0–53.6 °F) in the mountainous interior north to 17–19 °C (62.6–66.2 °F) in the south and on the [Guadiana](/wiki/Guadiana "Guadiana") [river basin](/wiki/River_basin "River basin") with variations from the highlands to the lowlands.[[138]](#cite_note-156) [Wildfires](/wiki/Wildfire "Wildfire") are a major issue, with Portugal having the highest percentage of burned area, on average, in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").[[139]](#cite_note-157)[[140]](#cite_note-158)[[141]](#cite_note-IEA_2021-159), and its coastal area being vulnerable to [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Portugal "Climate change in Portugal") and [severe weather](/wiki/Severe_weather "Severe weather").[[142]](#cite_note-160)[[141]](#cite_note-IEA_2021-159)[[143]](#cite_note-161)

### Biodiversity

Main article: [Biodiversity of Portugal](/wiki/Biodiversity_of_Portugal "Biodiversity of Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Miradouro_da_Cascata_do_Arado.jpg/250px-Miradouro_da_Cascata_do_Arado.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miradouro_da_Cascata_do_Arado.jpg)

The [Peneda-Gerês National Park](/wiki/Peneda-Ger%C3%AAs_National_Park "Peneda-Gerês National Park") in [Northern Portugal](/wiki/North_Region,_Portugal "North Region, Portugal") is the only national park in Portugal.[[144]](#cite_note-162)

Portugal is located on the [Mediterranean basin](/wiki/Mediterranean_basin "Mediterranean basin"), the 3rd most diverse hotspot of flora in the world.[[145]](#cite_note-florestas-163) It is home to 6 terrestrial ecoregions.[[s]](#cite_note-165) Over 22% of its land area is included in the [Natura 2000](/wiki/Natura_2000 "Natura 2000") network.[[147]](#cite_note-dre-166)[[145]](#cite_note-florestas-163) [Eucalyptus](/wiki/Eucalyptus "Eucalyptus"), [cork oak](/wiki/Cork_oak "Cork oak") and [maritime pine](/wiki/Maritime_pine "Maritime pine") together make up 71% of the total forested area of continental Portugal.[[148]](#cite_note-167)

Geographical and climatic conditions facilitate the introduction of exotic species that later turn to be invasive and destructive to the native habitats. Around 20% of the total number of extant species in continental Portugal are [exotic](/wiki/Introduced_species "Introduced species").[[149]](#cite_note-168) Portugal is the 2nd country in Europe with the highest number of threatened animal and plant species.[[150]](#cite_note-169)[[151]](#cite_note-170) Portugal as a whole is an important stopover for migratory bird species.[[152]](#cite_note-171)[[153]](#cite_note-172)

The large mammalian species of Portugal (deer, [Iberian ibex](/wiki/Iberian_ibex "Iberian ibex"), [wild boar](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar"), [red fox](/wiki/Red_fox "Red fox"), [Iberian wolf](/wiki/Iberian_wolf "Iberian wolf") and [Iberian lynx](/wiki/Iberian_lynx "Iberian lynx")) were once widespread throughout the country, but intense hunting, habitat degradation and growing pressure from agriculture and livestock reduced population on a large scale in the 19th and early 20th century, others, such as the [Portuguese ibex](/wiki/Portuguese_ibex "Portuguese ibex") were even led to extinction. Today, these animals are re-expanding their native range.[[154]](#cite_note-173)[[155]](#cite_note-mammals-174)

The Portuguese west coast is part of the four major Eastern Boundary [Upwelling](/wiki/Upwelling "Upwelling") Systems of the ocean.[[156]](#cite_note-175) This makes Portugal one of the largest per capita fish-consumers in the world.[[157]](#cite_note-176) 73% of the [freshwater fish](/wiki/Freshwater_fish "Freshwater fish") occurring in the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") are endemic, the largest out of any region in Europe.[[158]](#cite_note-177) Some [protected areas of Portugal](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Portugal "Protected areas of Portugal") include: the [Serras de Aire e Candeeiros](/wiki/Serras_de_Aire_e_Candeeiros_Natural_Park "Serras de Aire e Candeeiros Natural Park"),[[159]](#cite_note-178) the [Southwest Alentejo and Vicentine Coast Natural Park](/wiki/Southwest_Alentejo_and_Vicentine_Coast_Natural_Park "Southwest Alentejo and Vicentine Coast Natural Park"),[[160]](#cite_note-179) and the [Montesinho Natural Park](/wiki/Montesinho_Natural_Park "Montesinho Natural Park") which hosts some of the only populations of [Iberian wolf](/wiki/Iberian_wolf "Iberian wolf") and [Iberian brown bear](/wiki/Iberian_brown_bear "Iberian brown bear").[[161]](#cite_note-180)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Government of Portugal](/wiki/Government_of_Portugal "Government of Portugal") and [Politics of Portugal](/wiki/Politics_of_Portugal "Politics of Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Ant%C3%B3nioJos%C3%A9Seguro.png/250px-Ant%C3%B3nioJos%C3%A9Seguro.png)](/wiki/File:Ant%C3%B3nioJos%C3%A9Seguro.png)

[António José Seguro](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Jos%C3%A9_Seguro "António José Seguro")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_Portugal "President of Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Lu%C3%ADs_Montenegro%2C_2025.06.26_%2801%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Lu%C3%ADs_Montenegro%2C_2025.06.26_%2801%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lu%C3%ADs_Montenegro,_2025.06.26_(01)_(cropped).jpg)

[Luís Montenegro](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Montenegro "Luís Montenegro")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Portugal "Prime Minister of Portugal")

Portugal has been a [semi-presidential](/wiki/Semi-presidential_system "Semi-presidential system") representative democratic [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic") since the ratification of the [Constitution of 1976](/wiki/Portuguese_Constitution_of_1976 "Portuguese Constitution of 1976"), with [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon") as its capital.[[162]](#cite_note-181)[[163]](#cite_note-182) The Constitution grants the division or separation of powers among four sovereignty bodies: the [president](/wiki/President_of_Portugal "President of Portugal"), the [Assembly of the Republic](/wiki/Portuguese_parliament "Portuguese parliament"), the [government](/wiki/Government_of_Portugal "Government of Portugal") and the [courts](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Portugal "Judiciary of Portugal").[[164]](#cite_note-constitution-183)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Lisbon%2C_Bel%C3%A9m_Palace.JPG/250px-Lisbon%2C_Bel%C3%A9m_Palace.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lisbon,_Bel%C3%A9m_Palace.JPG)

The [Belém Palace](/wiki/Bel%C3%A9m_Palace "Belém Palace"), residence and workplace of the president

The head of state is the [president](/wiki/President_of_Portugal "President of Portugal"), elected to a five-year term by direct, [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage"), the incumbent is [António José Seguro](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Jos%C3%A9_Seguro "António José Seguro"). Although largely a ceremonial post,[[165]](#cite_note-184) presidential powers include the appointment of the prime minister and other members of the government, dismissing the prime minister, dissolving the parliament, [vetoing](/wiki/Veto "Veto") legislation[[t]](#cite_note-185) and declaring war.[[u]](#cite_note-186)[[166]](#cite_note-187) The president has also supervisory and [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_power "Reserve power") and is the *[ex officio](/wiki/Ex_officio "Ex officio")* [supreme commander](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the armed forces. The president is advised on issues of importance by the [Council of State](/wiki/Portuguese_Council_of_State "Portuguese Council of State").[[167]](#cite_note-188)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Pal%C3%A1cio_de_S%C3%A3o_Bento_%28Assembl%C3%A9e_nationale%29_%289307363528%29.jpg/250px-Pal%C3%A1cio_de_S%C3%A3o_Bento_%28Assembl%C3%A9e_nationale%29_%289307363528%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pal%C3%A1cio_de_S%C3%A3o_Bento_(Assembl%C3%A9e_nationale)_(9307363528).jpg)

The [São Bento Palace](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Bento_Palace "São Bento Palace"), seat of the [Assembly of the Republic](/wiki/Assembly_of_the_Republic_(Portugal) "Assembly of the Republic (Portugal)")

The [Assembly of the Republic](/wiki/Assembly_of_the_Republic_(Portugal) "Assembly of the Republic (Portugal)") is an [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") parliament composed of a maximum of 230 deputies elected for a four-year term.[[168]](#cite_note-189) The government is headed by the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Portugal "Prime Minister of Portugal") and includes ministers and secretaries of state, that have full executive powers;[[169]](#cite_note-190) the current prime minister is [Luís Montenegro](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Montenegro "Luís Montenegro").[[170]](#cite_note-191) The [Council of Ministers](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_(Portugal) "Council of Ministers (Portugal)") – under the prime minister[[v]](#cite_note-192) and the ministers – acts as the [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_(government) "Cabinet (government)").[[171]](#cite_note-193) The [courts](/wiki/Courts "Courts") are organised into several levels, among the judicial, administrative and fiscal branches. The [supreme courts](/wiki/Portuguese_Supreme_Court "Portuguese Supreme Court") are institutions of last appeal. The [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Portuguese_Constitutional_Court "Portuguese Constitutional Court") oversees the constitutionality of the laws.[[172]](#cite_note-194)

Portugal operates a multi-party system of competitive legislatures/local administrative governments at the national, regional and local levels. The Assembly of the Republic is dominated by three political parties, the [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_(Portugal) "Social Democratic Party (Portugal)") (PSD), [Chega](/wiki/Chega_(political_party) "Chega (political party)") (CH) and the [Socialist Party](/wiki/Socialist_Party_(Portugal) "Socialist Party (Portugal)") (PS), while the PSD and PS continue to be the dominant parties in the regional parliaments and at the [local level](/wiki/NUTS_statistical_regions_of_Portugal#LAUs "NUTS statistical regions of Portugal").[[w]](#cite_note-196) There has been recent trends towards [autocratization](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding "Democratic backsliding").[[174]](#cite_note-197)[[175]](#cite_note-198)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Portugal](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Portugal "Foreign relations of Portugal")

A member state of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") since 1955,[[109]](#cite_note-ONU-123) Portugal is a founding member of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") (1949),[[176]](#cite_note-199) the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") (1961)[[177]](#cite_note-200) and [EFTA](/wiki/European_Free_Trade_Association "European Free Trade Association") (1960);[[110]](#cite_note-EFTA-124) it left the last in 1986 to join the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community"), which became the European Union in 1993.[[124]](#cite_note-CEE-139) In 1996, Portugal co-founded the [Community of Portuguese Language Countries](/wiki/Community_of_Portuguese_Language_Countries "Community of Portuguese Language Countries") (CPLP), an international organisation and political association of [Lusophone](/wiki/Lusophone "Lusophone") nations where [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") is an official language.[[178]](#cite_note-201)

Portugal has hosted several international summits and events like the [first EU–Brazil summit](/wiki/1st_EU%E2%80%93Brazil_summit "1st EU–Brazil summit") in July 2007, the [second EU–African Union summit](/wiki/2nd_European_Union%E2%80%93African_Union_Summit "2nd European Union–African Union Summit") in December 2007, the signing of the [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Signing_of_the_Treaty_of_Lisbon "Signing of the Treaty of Lisbon") also in December 2007, and the [NATO summit](/wiki/2010_Lisbon_summit "2010 Lisbon summit") in November 2010. Portugal was a full member of the [Latin Union](/wiki/Latin_Union "Latin Union") (1983)[[179]](#cite_note-202) and the [Organisation of Ibero-American States](/wiki/Organization_of_Ibero-American_States "Organization of Ibero-American States") (1949).[[180]](#cite_note-203) It has a friendship alliance and [dual citizenship](/wiki/Multiple_citizenship "Multiple citizenship") treaty with Brazil.[[181]](#cite_note-204) Portugal and the United Kingdom share the world's oldest active military accord through their [Anglo-Portuguese Alliance](/wiki/Anglo-Portuguese_Alliance "Anglo-Portuguese Alliance"), [signed in 1386](/wiki/Treaty_of_Windsor_(1386) "Treaty of Windsor (1386)").[[62]](#cite_note-Windsor-71)

### Territorial disputes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Official_political_map_of_Portugal_of_2024.pdf/page1-250px-Official_political_map_of_Portugal_of_2024.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_political_map_of_Portugal_of_2024.pdf)

Official political map of Portugal, showing a section without a marked border with [Olivenza](/wiki/Olivenza "Olivenza") and its surrounding area[[x]](#cite_note-206)

[Olivenza](/wiki/Olivenza "Olivenza"): Under Portuguese sovereignty since 1297, the municipality of Olivença was ceded to Spain under the [Treaty of Badajoz](/wiki/Treaty_of_Badajoz_(1801) "Treaty of Badajoz (1801)") in 1801, after the [War of the Oranges](/wiki/War_of_the_Oranges "War of the Oranges"). Portugal claimed it back in 1815 under the [Treaty of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna"). However, since the 19th century, it has been continuously ruled by Spain which considers the territory theirs not only *de facto* but also *de jure*.[[183]](#cite_note-One-europe.net_2017-09-29-207)

[Savage Islands](/wiki/Savage_Islands "Savage Islands"): A small group of mostly uninhabited islets which fall under Portuguese Madeira's regional autonomous jurisdiction. Found in 1364 by Italian mariners under the service of [Prince Henry The Navigator](/wiki/Prince_Henry_The_Navigator "Prince Henry The Navigator"),[[184]](#cite_note-208) it was first noted by Portuguese navigator Diogo Gomes de Sintra in 1438. Historically, the islands have belonged to private Portuguese owners from the 16th century on, until 1971[[185]](#cite_note-209) when the government purchased them and established a natural reserve area covering the whole archipelago. The islands have been claimed by Spain since 1911,[[186]](#cite_note-210) and the dispute has caused some periods of political tension between the two countries.[[187]](#cite_note-211) The main problem for Spain's attempts to claim these small islands, has been not so much their intrinsic value, but the fact that they expand Portugal's [exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone "Exclusive economic zone") considerably to the south, in detriment of Spain.[[188]](#cite_note-Algarvedailynews.com_2017-09-29-212) The Selvagens Islands have been tentatively added to UNESCO's world heritage list in 2017.[[189]](#cite_note-213)

### Military

Main article: [Portuguese Armed Forces](/wiki/Portuguese_Armed_Forces "Portuguese Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/NRP_Bartolomeu_Dias_F333_in_Hernesaari_as_seen_from_Verkkokauppa_in_Helsinki%2C_Finland%2C_2023_April.jpg/250px-NRP_Bartolomeu_Dias_F333_in_Hernesaari_as_seen_from_Verkkokauppa_in_Helsinki%2C_Finland%2C_2023_April.jpg)](/wiki/File:NRP_Bartolomeu_Dias_F333_in_Hernesaari_as_seen_from_Verkkokauppa_in_Helsinki,_Finland,_2023_April.jpg)

[Portuguese Navy](/wiki/Portuguese_Navy "Portuguese Navy") frigate NRP Bartolomeu Dias

The [Portuguese Armed Forces](/wiki/Portuguese_Armed_Forces "Portuguese Armed Forces") have three branches commanded by the [Portuguese Armed Forces General Staff](/wiki/Armed_Forces_General_Staff_(Portugal) "Armed Forces General Staff (Portugal)") – [Navy](/wiki/Portuguese_Navy "Portuguese Navy"), [Army](/wiki/Portuguese_Army "Portuguese Army") and [Air Force](/wiki/Portuguese_Air_Force "Portuguese Air Force"). They serve primarily as a self-defence force whose mission is to protect the territorial integrity of the country but can also be used in offensive missions in foreign territories.[[190]](#cite_note-214) In recent years, the Portuguese military have carried out several [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") and [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") missions in various territories.[[y]](#cite_note-216) As of 2024, the three branches numbered 23,678 military personnel.[[192]](#cite_note-217) [Portuguese military budget](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures "List of countries by military expenditures") in 2023 was more than $4 billion, representing 1.48% of GDP.[[193]](#cite_note-218)

The Army of 10,969 personnel[[194]](#cite_note-FAPESS-219) comprises 3 brigades and other small units: an [infantry brigade](/wiki/Portuguese_Intervention_Brigade "Portuguese Intervention Brigade"),[[z]](#cite_note-220) a [mechanized brigade](/wiki/Portuguese_Mechanized_Brigade "Portuguese Mechanized Brigade")[[aa]](#cite_note-221) and a [rapid reaction brigade](/wiki/Portuguese_Rapid_Reaction_Brigade "Portuguese Rapid Reaction Brigade").[[ab]](#cite_note-222)[[195]](#cite_note-223) The Navy,[[ac]](#cite_note-226) the [world's oldest naval force](/wiki/List_of_navies "List of navies"),[[198]](#cite_note-227) has 5 frigates, 2 corvettes, 2 submarines, and 20 oceanic patrol vessels.[[199]](#cite_note-228) The Air Force[[ad]](#cite_note-229) has the [Lockheed F-16M Fighting Falcon](/wiki/Lockheed_F-16_Fighting_Falcon "Lockheed F-16 Fighting Falcon") as the main combat aircraft.[[200]](#cite_note-230)

In addition to the three branches of the armed forces, there is the [National Republican Guard](/wiki/National_Republican_Guard_(Portugal) "National Republican Guard (Portugal)"), a [gendarmerie](/wiki/Gendarmerie "Gendarmerie") force comprising 23,287 personnel by 2023,[[201]](#cite_note-231) under the authority of both the Defence and the Interior Ministry.[[202]](#cite_note-232) It has provided detachments for participation in international operations in Iraq and East Timor.[[203]](#cite_note-233) The United States maintains a military presence with 770 troops in the [Lajes Air Base](/wiki/Lajes_Air_Base "Lajes Air Base") at [Terceira Island](/wiki/Terceira_Island "Terceira Island"), in the Azores.[[204]](#cite_note-234)

### Law and justice

Main articles: [Law of Portugal](/wiki/Law_of_Portugal "Law of Portugal") and [Judiciary of Portugal](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Portugal "Judiciary of Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Inside_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_Justice_of_Portugal.jpg/250px-Inside_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_Justice_of_Portugal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inside_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_Justice_of_Portugal.jpg)

The courtroom of the [Supreme Court of Justice](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Justice_(Portugal) "Supreme Court of Justice (Portugal)"), in Lisbon

The Portuguese legal system is part of the [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") legal system. The main laws include the Constitution (1976), the [Portuguese Civil Code](/wiki/Portuguese_Civil_Code "Portuguese Civil Code") (1966) and the [Penal Code of Portugal](/wiki/Penal_Code_of_Portugal "Penal Code of Portugal") (1982), as amended. Other relevant laws are the Commercial Code (1888) and the Civil Procedure Code (1961). Portuguese laws were applied in the former [colonies and territories](/wiki/Portuguese_colonies "Portuguese colonies") and continue to be influences for those countries. The supreme national courts are the [Supreme Court of Justice](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Justice_(Portugal) "Supreme Court of Justice (Portugal)") and the [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Portugal "Constitutional Court of Portugal"). The [Public Ministry](/wiki/Public_Prosecution_Service_(Portugal) "Public Prosecution Service (Portugal)"), headed by the Attorney General of the Republic, constitutes the independent body of public prosecutors.[[205]](#cite_note-235)

In 2001, Portugal became the first country in the world to [decriminalize](/wiki/Drug_policy_of_Portugal "Drug policy of Portugal") personal possession and consumption of all drugs.[[206]](#cite_note-236)

#### Human rights

Main article: [Human rights in Portugal](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Portugal "Human rights in Portugal")

In 2003, Portugal added an anti-discrimination employment law on the basis of [sexual orientation](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_in_Portugal "LGBTQ rights in Portugal").[[207]](#cite_note-237) In 2004, sexual orientation was added to the Constitution as part of the protected from discrimination characteristics.[[208]](#cite_note-238) In 2010, Portugal became the sixth country in Europe and eighth in the world to legalise [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Portugal "Same-sex marriage in Portugal") at the national level.[[209]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-239)

[LGBT adoption](/wiki/LGBT_adoption "LGBT adoption") was legalized in 2016[[210]](#cite_note-240) as has female same-sex couple access to medically assisted reproduction.[[211]](#cite_note-241) In 2017 the *Law of Gender Identity*,[[212]](#cite_note-242) simplified the legal process of [gender](/wiki/Gender "Gender") and name change for [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") people, making it easier for minors to change their [sex marker](/wiki/Sex_marker "Sex marker") in legal documents.[[213]](#cite_note-243) In 2018, the right to [gender identity](/wiki/Gender_identity "Gender identity") and [gender expression](/wiki/Gender_expression "Gender expression") self-determination became protected, [intersex](/wiki/Intersex "Intersex") minors became protected by law from unnecessary [medical procedures](/wiki/Intersex_medical_interventions "Intersex medical interventions") "until the minor gender identity manifests" and the right of protection from discrimination on the basis of [sex characteristics](/wiki/Sex_characteristics_(legal_term) "Sex characteristics (legal term)") became protected by the same law.[[214]](#cite_note-244)

### Law enforcement

Main articles: [Law enforcement in Portugal](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Portugal "Law enforcement in Portugal") and [Crime in Portugal](/wiki/Crime_in_Portugal "Crime in Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Pol%C3%ADcia_Judici%C3%A1ria_%28Portugal%29_headquarters%2C_Lisbon_04.jpg/250px-Pol%C3%ADcia_Judici%C3%A1ria_%28Portugal%29_headquarters%2C_Lisbon_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pol%C3%ADcia_Judici%C3%A1ria_(Portugal)_headquarters,_Lisbon_04.jpg)

The headquarters of *[Polícia Judiciária](/wiki/Pol%C3%ADcia_Judici%C3%A1ria "Polícia Judiciária")* (PJ), in [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon")

Portugal's main police organisations are the *[Guarda Nacional Republicana](/wiki/Guarda_Nacional_Republicana "Guarda Nacional Republicana") – GNR* ([National Republican Guard](/wiki/National_Republican_Guard_(Portugal) "National Republican Guard (Portugal)")), a [gendarmerie](/wiki/Gendarmerie "Gendarmerie"); the *[Polícia de Segurança Pública](/wiki/Pol%C3%ADcia_de_Seguran%C3%A7a_P%C3%BAblica "Polícia de Segurança Pública") – PSP* (Public Security Police), a civilian police force who work in urban areas; and the *[Polícia Judiciária](/wiki/Pol%C3%ADcia_Judici%C3%A1ria "Polícia Judiciária") – PJ* (Judicial Police), a highly specialised criminal investigation police that is overseen by the Public Ministry.[[215]](#cite_note-245)

Portugal has [49 correctional facilities](/w/index.php?title=List_of_prisons_in_Portugal&action=edit&redlink=1 "List of prisons in Portugal (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lista_de_pris%C3%B5es_portuguesas "pt:Lista de prisões portuguesas")] run by the [Directorate-General for Reintegration and Prison Services](/w/index.php?title=Directorate-General_for_Reintegration_and_Prison_Services&action=edit&redlink=1 "Directorate-General for Reintegration and Prison Services (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dire%C3%A7%C3%A3o-Geral_de_Reinser%C3%A7%C3%A3o_e_Servi%C3%A7os_Prisionais "pt:Direção-Geral de Reinserção e Serviços Prisionais")] (DGRSP).[[216]](#cite_note-246) They include seventeen central prisons, four special prisons, twenty-seven regional prisons, and one 'Cadeia de Apoio' (Support Detention Centre).[[217]](#cite_note-247) As of 1 January 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Portugal&action=edit), the prison population stood at 12,193 inmates, which comes to about 0.11% of the country's entire population.[[218]](#cite_note-248) The incarceration rate had a strong increase after 2010, with the prison population surpassing 14,000 inmates by 2013, but after that same year the incarceration rate began to steadily decline, a trend that has continued in recent years.[[219]](#cite_note-prisons_2021-249)[[220]](#cite_note-250)

### Subdivisions

#### Provinces

Main article: [Provinces of Portugal](/wiki/Provinces_of_Portugal "Provinces of Portugal")

The term *provinces* ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *províncias*) has been used throughout history to identify regions of [continental Portugal](/wiki/Continental_Portugal "Continental Portugal"). Current legal [subdivisions of Portugal](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Portugal "Subdivisions of Portugal") do not coincide with the provinces, but several provinces, in their 19th- and 20th-century versions, still correspond to culturally relevant, strongly self-identifying categories. They include:

* [Alentejo](/wiki/Alentejo "Alentejo") ([Alto Alentejo](/wiki/Alto_Alentejo_Province "Alto Alentejo Province"), [Baixo Alentejo](/wiki/Baixo_Alentejo_Province "Baixo Alentejo Province"))
* [Algarve](/wiki/Algarve "Algarve")
* [Beira](/wiki/Beira_(Portugal) "Beira (Portugal)") ([Beira Alta](/wiki/Beira_Alta_Province "Beira Alta Province"), [Beira Baixa](/wiki/Beira_Baixa_Province "Beira Baixa Province"), [Beira Litoral](/wiki/Beira_Litoral_Province "Beira Litoral Province"))
* [Douro Litoral](/wiki/Douro_Litoral_Province "Douro Litoral Province")
* [Estremadura](/wiki/Estremadura_Province_(1936%E2%80%931976) "Estremadura Province (1936–1976)")
* [Minho](/wiki/Minho_Province "Minho Province")
* [Ribatejo](/wiki/Ribatejo_Province "Ribatejo Province")
* [Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro](/wiki/Tr%C3%A1s-os-Montes_e_Alto_Douro_Province "Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro Province")

The islands of [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores") and [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira") were not called "provinces".[[221]](#cite_note-251)

#### Districts, regions and municipalities

Main article: [Administrative divisions of Portugal](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Portugal "Administrative divisions of Portugal")

Continental Portugal is agglomerated into 18 districts, while the archipelagos of the Azores and Madeira are governed as [autonomous regions](/wiki/Autonomous_area "Autonomous area"); the largest units, established since 1976, are either mainland Portugal and the [autonomous regions of Portugal](/wiki/Autonomous_regions_of_Portugal "Autonomous regions of Portugal") ([Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores") and [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira")).[[222]](#cite_note-252) Administratively, Portugal is divided into 308 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Portugal "Municipalities of Portugal") (*municípios* or *[concelhos](/wiki/Concelhos "Concelhos")*). Operationally, the municipalities (and civil parishes), along with the national government, are the only legally [local administrative units](/wiki/Local_administrative_unit "Local administrative unit") identified by the government of Portugal.[[223]](#cite_note-253)
