Within the European Union NUTS system, Portugal is divided into nine regions:[[229]](#cite_note-Official_Journal_2023-259) the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores"), [Alentejo](/wiki/Alentejo "Alentejo"), [Algarve](/wiki/Algarve "Algarve"), [Centro](/wiki/Centro,_Portugal "Centro, Portugal"), [Lisboa](/wiki/Lisboa_Region "Lisboa Region"), [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira"), [Norte](/wiki/Norte,_Portugal "Norte, Portugal"), [Oeste e Vale do Tejo](/wiki/Oeste_e_Vale_do_Tejo "Oeste e Vale do Tejo") and [Península de Setúbal](/wiki/Pen%C3%ADnsula_de_Set%C3%BAbal "Península de Setúbal"), and with the exception of the Azores and Madeira, NUTS areas are subdivided into 24 subregions.[[230]](#cite_note-260)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Portugal](/wiki/Economy_of_Portugal "Economy of Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Parque_das_Na%C3%A7%C3%B5es_31.jpg/250px-Parque_das_Na%C3%A7%C3%B5es_31.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parque_das_Na%C3%A7%C3%B5es_31.jpg)

[Vasco da Gama Tower](/wiki/Vasco_da_Gama_Tower "Vasco da Gama Tower") in [Parque das Nações](/wiki/Parque_das_Na%C3%A7%C3%B5es "Parque das Nações") with the [Vasco da Gama Bridge](/wiki/Vasco_da_Gama_Bridge "Vasco da Gama Bridge"), the longest bridge in the [EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"),[[231]](#cite_note-261) in [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon")

Portugal is a member of the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") with a GDP per capita of 82% of the EU27 average in 2024,[[232]](#cite_note-262) and a [HDI](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index") of 0.890 (the 40th highest in the world) in 2023.[[13]](#cite_note-UNHDR-17)[[233]](#cite_note-263) It holds the [14th largest gold reserve in the world](/wiki/Gold_reserve "Gold reserve") at its [national central bank](/wiki/Bank_of_Portugal "Bank of Portugal"), with the highest gold share of [forex reserves](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_reserves "Foreign exchange reserves") in the world,[[234]](#cite_note-264) has the 8th largest reserves of [lithium](/wiki/Lithium "Lithium"),[[235]](#cite_note-265)[[236]](#cite_note-266)[[237]](#cite_note-267) and total exports representing 46.6% of its GDP in 2024.[[238]](#cite_note-268)

Portugal's national currency is the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") (€), which started transitioning from the [Portuguese Escudo](/wiki/Portuguese_Escudo "Portuguese Escudo") in 2000 and consolidated in 2002. Most industries, businesses and financial institutions are concentrated in the [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon_metropolitan_area "Lisbon metropolitan area") and [Porto](/wiki/Greater_Metropolitan_Area_of_Porto "Greater Metropolitan Area of Porto") metropolitan areas, plus the areas around these two metro areas.[[239]](#cite_note-269)

Since the 1990s, Portugal's economic model has been based on [public consumption](/wiki/Consumption_(economics) "Consumption (economics)") and [economic development](/wiki/Economic_development "Economic development") focused on exports, private [investment](/wiki/Investment_(macroeconomics) "Investment (macroeconomics)") and the development of its [high-tech](/wiki/High-tech "High-tech") sector. Consequently, business services have overtaken more traditional industries such as textiles, clothing, footwear and [cork](/wiki/Cork_(material) "Cork (material)") (Portugal is the world's leading cork producer),[[240]](#cite_note-270) wood products and beverages, like [wine](/wiki/Wine "Wine") and [beer](/wiki/Beer_in_Portugal "Beer in Portugal").[[241]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-271)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Veh%C3%ADculos_en_el_puerto_de_Set%C3%BAbal%2C_Portugal%2C_2019-05-24%2C_DD_01.jpg/250px-Veh%C3%ADculos_en_el_puerto_de_Set%C3%BAbal%2C_Portugal%2C_2019-05-24%2C_DD_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Veh%C3%ADculos_en_el_puerto_de_Set%C3%BAbal,_Portugal,_2019-05-24,_DD_01.jpg)

[Volkswagen Autoeuropa](/wiki/Volkswagen_Autoeuropa "Volkswagen Autoeuropa") cars in the Port of Setúbal

In 2001, the gross agricultural product accounted for 4% of the economy; in 2022 it was 2%.[[242]](#cite_note-272) According to [FAO](/wiki/FAO "FAO"), Portugal is the top producer of [cork](/wiki/Cork_(material) "Cork (material)") and [carob](/wiki/Carob "Carob") in the world, accounting for about 50% and 30% of world production, respectively.[[243]](#cite_note-273) It is the third largest exporter of [chestnuts](/wiki/Chestnut "Chestnut") and third largest European producer of [pulp](/wiki/Pulp_(paper) "Pulp (paper)").[[244]](#cite_note-274) Portugal is among the world's top ten largest [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil") producers and fourth largest exporter.[[245]](#cite_note-275)

Portugal hosts the [Euronext Lisbon](/wiki/Euronext_Lisbon "Euronext Lisbon") [stock exchange](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange") which includes [EDP](/wiki/Energias_de_Portugal "Energias de Portugal"), [Galp](/wiki/Galp_Energia "Galp Energia"), [Jerónimo Martins](/wiki/Jer%C3%B3nimo_Martins "Jerónimo Martins"), [Mota-Engil](/wiki/Mota-Engil "Mota-Engil"), Novabase, [Semapa](/wiki/Semapa "Semapa"), [Portucel Soporcel](/wiki/Portucel_Soporcel "Portucel Soporcel"), [Portugal Telecom](/wiki/Portugal_Telecom "Portugal Telecom") and [Sonae](/wiki/Sonae "Sonae").[[246]](#cite_note-276)

### Science and Technology

Main article: [Science and technology in Portugal](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Portugal "Science and technology in Portugal")

The [European Innovation Scoreboard](/wiki/European_Innovation_Scoreboard "European Innovation Scoreboard") 2025, placed Portugal-based innovation 16th, with increase in R&D government support and resource produtivity.[[247]](#cite_note-277) Portugal was ranked 31st in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[248]](#cite_note-278) Among the largest non-state-run research institutions are the [Instituto Gulbenkian de Ciência](/wiki/Instituto_Gulbenkian_de_Ci%C3%AAncia "Instituto Gulbenkian de Ciência"), the [International Iberian Nanotechnology Laboratory](/wiki/International_Iberian_Nanotechnology_Laboratory "International Iberian Nanotechnology Laboratory") and the [Champalimaud Foundation](/wiki/Champalimaud_Foundation "Champalimaud Foundation"), one of the leading research centres for [neuroscience](/wiki/Neuroscience "Neuroscience") and [oncology](/wiki/Oncology "Oncology") in the world.[[249]](#cite_note-279)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Portugal](/wiki/Tourism_in_Portugal "Tourism in Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Praia_da_Marinha_%2835518737040%29.jpg/250px-Praia_da_Marinha_%2835518737040%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Praia_da_Marinha_(35518737040).jpg)

Coast in [Algarve](/wiki/Algarve "Algarve")

Portugal is among the [top 20 most-visited countries in the world](/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings"), receiving nearly 29 million foreign tourists by 2024.[[250]](#cite_note-280) In 2014, Portugal was elected *The Best European Country* by *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")*.[[251]](#cite_note-281) In 2017, Portugal was elected both *Europe's Leading Destination*[[252]](#cite_note-282) and in 2018 and 2019, *World's Leading Destination*[[253]](#cite_note-283)

Travel and [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Portugal "Tourism in Portugal") is an important part of Portugal's economy. As of 2024, the tourism sector contributed 11.9% to GDP, worth €34 billion, with tourism contributing 0.3% to an annual GDP growth of 1.9%.[[254]](#cite_note-284)

Popular tourism destinations include: [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon"), [Cascais](/wiki/Cascais "Cascais"), [Algarve](/wiki/Algarve "Algarve"), [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira"), [Nazaré](/wiki/Nazar%C3%A9,_Portugal "Nazaré, Portugal"), [Fátima](/wiki/F%C3%A1tima,_Portugal "Fátima, Portugal"), [Óbidos](/wiki/%C3%93bidos,_Portugal "Óbidos, Portugal"), [Porto](/wiki/Porto "Porto"), [Braga](/wiki/Braga "Braga"), [Guimarães](/wiki/Guimar%C3%A3es "Guimarães") and [Coimbra](/wiki/Coimbra "Coimbra"). Lisbon is the 9th most visited city in Europe,[[255]](#cite_note-285) and with 6,7 million tourists occupying the city's hotels by 2024, of which 5,5 million were foreign.[[256]](#cite_note-286)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Portugal](/wiki/Transport_in_Portugal "Transport in Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/T%C3%BAnel_do_Mar%C3%A3o.jpg/250px-T%C3%BAnel_do_Mar%C3%A3o.jpg)](/wiki/File:T%C3%BAnel_do_Mar%C3%A3o.jpg)

The [Marão Tunnel](/w/index.php?title=Mar%C3%A3o_Tunnel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Marão Tunnel (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/T%C3%BAnel_do_Mar%C3%A3o "pt:Túnel do Marão")] is the longest road tunnel in the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula").[[257]](#cite_note-287)

Portugal has a 68,732 km (42,708 mi) road network, of which 3,065 km (1,905 mi) are part of [system of 48 motorways](/wiki/Roads_in_Portugal#Motorways "Roads in Portugal"), making it the [8th largest motorway system among 42 European countries](/wiki/Evolution_of_motorway_construction_in_European_nations "Evolution of motorway construction in European nations").

By 2024, Portugal had 37 civil airports,[[258]](#cite_note-288) of which five are the main international airports, three located in the mainland: [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon_Airport "Lisbon Airport") ([the busiest in the country](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Portugal "List of the busiest airports in Portugal")), [Porto](/wiki/Porto_Airport "Porto Airport"), and [Faro](/wiki/Faro_Airport "Faro Airport") airports; while the [Funchal](/wiki/Madeira_Airport "Madeira Airport") airport is located in [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira"), and the [Ponta Delgada](/wiki/John_Paul_II_Ponta_Delgada_Airport "John Paul II Ponta Delgada Airport") airport in the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores").[[259]](#cite_note-289)

A national railway system that extends throughout mainland Portugal and into Spain, is supported and administered by [Comboios de Portugal](/wiki/Comboios_de_Portugal "Comboios de Portugal") (CP).[[260]](#cite_note-290) [Rail transport](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") of passengers and goods is derived using the 2,527 km (1,570 mi) of railway lines currently in service,[[261]](#cite_note-291) of which 1,791 km (1,113 mi) are electrified.[[262]](#cite_note-CP-292)

The major seaports are located in [Sines](/wiki/Port_of_Sines "Port of Sines"), [Leixões](/wiki/Leix%C3%B5es "Leixões"), [Lisbon](/wiki/Port_of_Lisbon "Port of Lisbon"), [Setúbal](/wiki/Set%C3%BAbal "Setúbal"), [Aveiro](/wiki/Aveiro,_Portugal "Aveiro, Portugal"), [Figueira da Foz](/wiki/Figueira_da_Foz "Figueira da Foz"), and [Faro](/wiki/Faro,_Portugal "Faro, Portugal").[[263]](#cite_note-293) The two largest metropolitan areas (Lisbon and Porto) have subway and tram systems.

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Portugal](/wiki/Energy_in_Portugal "Energy in Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Central_Offshore_Windfloat_Atlantic_-_Portugal_%2853426345383%29.jpg/500px-Central_Offshore_Windfloat_Atlantic_-_Portugal_%2853426345383%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Central_Offshore_Windfloat_Atlantic_-_Portugal_(53426345383).jpg)

Windfloat Atlantic [floating wind turbines](/wiki/Floating_wind_turbine "Floating wind turbine") near [Viana do Castelo](/wiki/Viana_do_Castelo "Viana do Castelo")[[264]](#cite_note-294)

As of 2023, oil made up 44% of Portugal's total energy supply. However, the country phased out coal-fired generation in 2021 and has been developing [renewable energies](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") such as [hydopower](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") and [wind power](/wiki/Wind_power "Wind power")[[265]](#cite_note-295) and investing in public transport and [electric vehicles](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle").[[266]](#cite_note-296)[[267]](#cite_note-297)

Portugal has considerable wind and hydropower resources. In 2006, the world's then largest [solar power](/wiki/Solar_energy "Solar energy") plant, the [Moura Photovoltaic Power Station](/wiki/Moura_Photovoltaic_Power_Station "Moura Photovoltaic Power Station"), began operating, while the world's first commercial [wave power](/wiki/Wave_power "Wave power") farm, the [Aguçadoura Wave Farm](/wiki/Agu%C3%A7adoura_Wave_Farm "Aguçadoura Wave Farm"), opened in 2008 near [Póvoa de Varzim](/wiki/P%C3%B3voa_de_Varzim "Póvoa de Varzim"), being, however, shutdown a short time later.[[268]](#cite_note-298) There are also offshore [floating wind turbine](/wiki/Floating_wind_turbine "Floating wind turbine") projects, mainly in the coast of Viana do Castelo.[[269]](#cite_note-299) In 2025, renewable energy resources were producing 68% of the nation's electricity.[[270]](#cite_note-300) Since 2021, electricity imports, mainly from Spain, have outnumbered exports by around 20% as of 2023,[[271]](#cite_note-301) with most of the energy coming from renewable sources.[[272]](#cite_note-302)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Portugal](/wiki/Demographics_of_Portugal "Demographics of Portugal")

See also: [Portuguese people](/wiki/Portuguese_people "Portuguese people")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Mapa_densidade_portugal.png/250px-Mapa_densidade_portugal.png)](/wiki/File:Mapa_densidade_portugal.png)

Population density in [Continental Portugal](/wiki/Continental_Portugal "Continental Portugal") by statistical area per km2:[[273]](#cite_note-303)

List

* 0-49
* 50-99
* 100-499
* 500-999
* 1000-1999
* 2000+

As of 31 December 2024, Portugal had a population 10,749,635, of which 52.2% was female and 47.8% male, according to [Statistics Portugal](/wiki/Instituto_Nacional_de_Estat%C3%ADstica_(Portugal) "Instituto Nacional de Estatística (Portugal)").[[10]](#cite_note-Stat_Portugal-14)[[274]](#cite_note-INEPop-304) In 2025, the median life expectancy reached 82.95 years,[[275]](#cite_note-305) with United Nations projecting a rise of up to 90 years or more by 2100.[[276]](#cite_note-306) The population historically has been relatively homogeneous, with most people adhering to [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_church "Catholic church") and speaking [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language").[[277]](#cite_note-Censos2021-307)[[278]](#cite_note-308)

Portugal has had a [fertility rate below the replacement rate of 2.1](/wiki/Sub-replacement_fertility_rate "Sub-replacement fertility rate") since the 1980s.[[279]](#cite_note-309) The [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") (TFR) as of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Portugal&action=edit) was estimated at 1.36 children born per woman, one of the lowest in the world,[[280]](#cite_note-310)[[281]](#cite_note-311) and considerably below the high of 5.0 in 1911.[[282]](#cite_note-312) Consequently, Portugal's population has been steadily ageing and was the 11th oldest in the world in 2024, with a median age of 46.4 years and the fourth highest number of citizens over 65 years, at 21.8% of the total population.[[26]](#cite_note-CIA-34)[[283]](#cite_note-313) According to projections by the national statistics office, the population will fall to 7.7 million by 2080 and the population will continue to age.[[284]](#cite_note-314) As of 2022, 60.2% of births were to unmarried women,[[285]](#cite_note-315) and 24.5% of births were to foreign born women.[[286]](#cite_note-316)

Portuguese society displays relatively high rates of socioeconomic equality, with the country ranking 24th within the 41 countries of the EU and [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") in the 2019 [Social Justice Index](/wiki/Social_justice_index "Social justice index").[[287]](#cite_note-317) In 2018, Portugal's parliament approved a budget plan for 2019 that included tax breaks for returning emigrants in a bid to attract back those who left during the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis").[[288]](#cite_note-318)

According to a Statistics Portugal study conducted between 2022 and 2023, 6.4 million people aged between 18 and 74 years old identified themselves as White (84.2%), more than 262,000 as Mixed-race (3.4%), nearly 169,200 as Black (2.2%), 56,600 as Asian (0.7%), and 47,500 as [Romani](/wiki/Romani_people_in_Portugal "Romani people in Portugal") (0.6%).[[1]](#cite_note-INESURVEY-1)[[289]](#cite_note-INERace-319)[[290]](#cite_note-320)

### Immigration

Main article: [Immigration to Portugal](/wiki/Immigration_to_Portugal "Immigration to Portugal")

Historically a country of [emigration](/wiki/Emigration "Emigration"),[[291]](#cite_note-diaspora-321) Portugal has been a [net recipient of immigrants](/wiki/Net_migration_rate "Net migration rate") since the early 21st century.[[292]](#cite_note-322) As of 2024, legal resident foreigners number 1,543,697, or approximately 14% of the population;[[293]](#cite_note-aima-323)[[294]](#cite_note-324) these figures do not include more than 340,000 resident foreigners who acquired [Portuguese citizenship](/wiki/Portuguese_nationality_law "Portuguese nationality law") between 2008 and 2022—and thus constitute around 3.27% of the country's population in 2022.[[295]](#cite_note-325) That year, almost 21,000 foreign residents acquired [Portuguese citizenship](/wiki/Portuguese_passport "Portuguese passport"), of which 11,170 were female and 9,674 were male.[[296]](#cite_note-326)

### Urbanisation

Based on commuting patterns, OECD and Eurostat define eight [metropolitan areas of Portugal](/wiki/Metropolitan_areas_of_Portugal "Metropolitan areas of Portugal").[[297]](#cite_note-327) Only two have populations over 1 million, and since the 2013 local government reform, these are the only two which also have administrative legal status of metropolitan areas: [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon_metropolitan_area "Lisbon metropolitan area") and [Porto](/wiki/Greater_Metropolitan_Area_of_Porto "Greater Metropolitan Area of Porto"),[[298]](#cite_note-law75-328)[[299]](#cite_note-Oliveira-329) Several smaller metropolitan areas ([Algarve](/wiki/Greater_Metropolitan_Area_of_the_Algarve "Greater Metropolitan Area of the Algarve"), [Aveiro](/wiki/Greater_Metropolitan_Area_of_Aveiro "Greater Metropolitan Area of Aveiro"), [Coimbra](/wiki/Greater_Metropolitan_Area_of_Coimbra "Greater Metropolitan Area of Coimbra"), [Minho](/wiki/Greater_Metropolitan_Area_of_Minho "Greater Metropolitan Area of Minho") and [Viseu](/wiki/Greater_Metropolitan_Area_of_Viseu "Greater Metropolitan Area of Viseu"))[[299]](#cite_note-Oliveira-329) also held this status from 2003 to 2008, when they were converted into [intermunicipal communities](/wiki/Intermunicipal_communities_of_Portugal "Intermunicipal communities of Portugal"), whose territories are roughly based on the [NUTS III](/wiki/Nomenclature_of_Territorial_Units_for_Statistics "Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics") statistical regions.[[300]](#cite_note-law45-330)[[299]](#cite_note-Oliveira-329)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Portugal](/wiki/Religion_in_Portugal "Religion in Portugal")

Religious affiliation in Portugal, according to the 2021 census:[[277]](#cite_note-Censos2021-307)

1. [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") (80.2%)
2. [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") (2.10%)
3. Other Christian (2.40%)
4. Other religion (1.13%)
5. [No religion](/wiki/Irreligious "Irreligious") (14.1%)

Portugal is a [secular state](/wiki/Secular_state "Secular state") [since 1911](/w/index.php?title=Law_on_the_separation_of_church_and_state_(Portugal)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Law on the separation of church and state (Portugal) (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lei_da_Separa%C3%A7%C3%A3o_do_Estado_das_Igrejas "pt:Lei da Separação do Estado das Igrejas")],[[302]](#cite_note-332) and it guarantees [religious freedom](/wiki/Religious_freedom "Religious freedom").[[ae]](#cite_note-335) Despite of not having any [official religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion"), the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Portugal "Catholic Church in Portugal") has a long history in the country.[[305]](#cite_note-336)[[306]](#cite_note-337)

According to the 2021 Census, 80.2% of the Portuguese population was Catholic, while 14.7% are nonreligious.[[277]](#cite_note-Censos2021-307) The country has small [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism_in_Portugal "Protestantism in Portugal"), [Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints_in_Portugal "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints in Portugal"), [Muslim](/wiki/Islam_in_Portugal "Islam in Portugal"), [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Portugal "Hinduism in Portugal"), [Sikh](/wiki/Sikhism_in_Portugal "Sikhism in Portugal"), [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), [Jehovah's Witnesses](/w/index.php?title=Jehovah%27s_Witnesses_in_Portugal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jehovah's Witnesses in Portugal (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Testemunhas_de_Jeov%C3%A1_em_Portugal "pt:Testemunhas de Jeová em Portugal")], [Baháʼí](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith_in_Portugal "Baháʼí Faith in Portugal"), [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Portugal "Buddhism in Portugal"), [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism_in_Portugal "Judaism in Portugal") and [Spiritist](/wiki/Kardecist_spiritism "Kardecist spiritism") communities. Influences from [African Traditional Religion](/wiki/African_Traditional_Religion "African Traditional Religion") and Chinese Traditional Religion are also felt among many people, particularly in fields related with Traditional Chinese Medicine and Traditional African Herbal Medicine.[[af]](#cite_note-339)

There are 13 [calendar dates](/wiki/Calendar_date "Calendar date") designated by the Portuguese government as [national holidays](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Portugal "Public holidays in Portugal"), seven of which celebrate events of the [Christian calendar](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year").[[308]](#cite_note-340) Many Portuguese holidays, festivals and traditions have a Christian origin or connotation, and most have high levels of participation by the local people.[[309]](#cite_note-341)

Even though Portugal has deep ties with Christianity, as of 2019, the majority of its people had shown to be tolerant towards followers of other faiths, with the [Muslim community](/wiki/Islam_in_Portugal "Islam in Portugal") perceiving itself as thoroughly integrated into Portugal and believing that the country provided conducive conditions for smooth integration.[[310]](#cite_note-342)

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Portugal](/wiki/Languages_of_Portugal "Languages of Portugal")

See also: [Portuguese language](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Gen%C3%ADsio04.jpg/250px-Gen%C3%ADsio04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gen%C3%ADsio04.jpg)

A sign in [Mirandese](/wiki/Mirandese_language "Mirandese language") in [Miranda do Douro](/wiki/Miranda_do_Douro "Miranda do Douro")

Portuguese is the official language of Portugal. [Mirandese](/wiki/Mirandese_language "Mirandese language") is also recognised as a co-official regional language in some municipalities of North-Eastern Portugal. It is part of the [Astur-Leonese](/wiki/Astur-Leonese "Astur-Leonese") group of languages.[[311]](#cite_note-343) An estimate of between 6,000 and 7,000 Mirandese speakers has been documented for Portugal.[[312]](#cite_note-344) Furthermore, a particular dialect known as [Barranquenho](/wiki/Barranquenho "Barranquenho"), spoken in [Barrancos](/wiki/Barrancos "Barrancos"), is also officially recognised and protected in Portugal since 2021.[[313]](#cite_note-345) [Minderico](/wiki/Minderico_language "Minderico language"), a [sociolect](/wiki/Sociolect "Sociolect") of the Portuguese language, is spoken by around 500 people in the town of [Minde](/wiki/Minde,_Portugal "Minde, Portugal").[[314]](#cite_note-346)

According to the [International English Proficiency Index](/wiki/EF_English_Proficiency_Index "EF English Proficiency Index"), Portugal has a high proficiency level in English, higher than those of other Romance-speaking European countries like France, Italy or Spain.[[315]](#cite_note-347)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Portugal](/wiki/Education_in_Portugal "Education in Portugal")

The educational system is divided into preschool (for those under age six), basic education (nine years, in three stages, compulsory), secondary education (three years, compulsory since 2010), and higher education (subdivided in university and [polytechnic](/wiki/Polytechnic_(Portugal) "Polytechnic (Portugal)") education). Universities are usually organised into [faculties](/wiki/Faculty_(division) "Faculty (division)"). Institutes and schools are also common designations for autonomous subdivisions of [Portuguese higher education institutions](/wiki/List_of_universities_and_colleges_in_Portugal "List of universities and colleges in Portugal").[[316]](#cite_note-348)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Coimbra_BW_2018-10-06_10-11-56.jpg/250px-Coimbra_BW_2018-10-06_10-11-56.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coimbra_BW_2018-10-06_10-11-56.jpg)

The [University of Coimbra](/wiki/University_of_Coimbra "University of Coimbra") in [Coimbra](/wiki/Coimbra "Coimbra") is the first university in Portugal.

Portuguese universities have existed since 1290. The [oldest Portuguese university](/wiki/University_of_Coimbra "University of Coimbra")[[317]](#cite_note-349) was first established in Lisbon before moving to [Coimbra](/wiki/Coimbra "Coimbra"). Historically, within the scope of the Portuguese Empire, the Portuguese founded the oldest engineering school of the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") (the *[Real Academia de Artilharia, Fortificação e Desenho](/wiki/Real_Academia_de_Artilharia,_Fortifica%C3%A7%C3%A3o_e_Desenho "Real Academia de Artilharia, Fortificação e Desenho")* of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro")) in 1792, as well as the oldest medical college in Asia (the *[Escola Médico-Cirúrgica](/wiki/Escola_M%C3%A9dico-Cir%C3%BArgica_de_Goa "Escola Médico-Cirúrgica de Goa")* of [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa")) in 1842. Presently, the largest university in Portugal is the [University of Lisbon](/wiki/University_of_Lisbon "University of Lisbon").[[318]](#cite_note-350)

The [Bologna process](/wiki/Bologna_process "Bologna process") has been adopted by Portuguese universities and poly-technical institutes in 2006. Higher education in state-run educational establishments is provided on a competitive basis, a system of *[numerus clausus](/wiki/Numerus_clausus "Numerus clausus")* is enforced through a national database on student admissions. However, every higher education institution offers also a number of additional vacant places through other admission processes for sportsmen, mature applicants (over 23 years old), [international students](/wiki/International_students "International students"), foreign students from the [Lusosphere](/wiki/Lusosphere "Lusosphere"), degree owners from other institutions, students from other institutions ([academic transfer](/wiki/Transfer_admissions_in_the_United_States "Transfer admissions in the United States")), former students (readmission), and course change, which are subject to specific standards and regulations set by each institution or course department.[[319]](#cite_note-351)

### Health

Main article: [Health in Portugal](/wiki/Health_in_Portugal "Health in Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Hospital_de_Santa_Maria.jpg/250px-Hospital_de_Santa_Maria.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hospital_de_Santa_Maria.jpg)

The [Santa Maria Hospital](/wiki/Hospital_de_Santa_Maria "Hospital de Santa Maria") in [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon") is the largest public hospital in Portugal.[[320]](#cite_note-352)

In 2025, Portugal ranked as 23rd best healthcare system in the world.[[321]](#cite_note-353) The health system is characterised by three coexisting systems: the National Health Service (*Serviço Nacional de Saúde*, SNS), special social health insurance schemes for certain professions (health subsystems) and voluntary private health insurance. The SNS provides universal coverage. In addition, about 55% of the population is covered by the health subsystems,[[322]](#cite_note-354) 43% by private insurance schemes and another 12% by mutual funds.[[323]](#cite_note-355)[[324]](#cite_note-356)

Similarly to other 'Eur-A countries', (Western Europe), most Portuguese die from [noncommunicable diseases](/wiki/Noncommunicable_diseases "Noncommunicable diseases").[[325]](#cite_note-Saude-357) Portugal's [infant mortality rate](/wiki/Infant_mortality_rate "Infant mortality rate") (IMR) stood at 2,25 deaths per 1,000 live births as of 2024.[[326]](#cite_note-358) A Eurostat opinion-poll in 2023 found that 55.4% of adults rated their health as good or very good, the third lowest rate collected in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").[[327]](#cite_note-359) The largest university hospital in the country is [Hospital de Santa Maria](/wiki/Hospital_de_Santa_Maria "Hospital de Santa Maria"), Lisbon.[[328]](#cite_note-360)

Despite good economic development, the average Portuguese, along with Italians, have been among the shortest in Europe since around 1890 and still are.[[329]](#cite_note-361) A driving factor was modest real wage growth, given late industrialisation and economic growth compared to the European core. Another determinant was delayed [human capital](/wiki/Human_capital "Human capital") formation.[[330]](#cite_note-362)

## Culture

Main articles: [Culture of Portugal](/wiki/Culture_of_Portugal "Culture of Portugal") and [Mass media in Portugal](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Portugal "Mass media in Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Feira_de_Barcelos_%2805Set%29_040.jpg/250px-Feira_de_Barcelos_%2805Set%29_040.jpg)](/wiki/File:Feira_de_Barcelos_(05Set)_040.jpg)

[Rooster of Barcelos](/wiki/Rooster_of_Barcelos "Rooster of Barcelos"), the iconic Portuguese souvenir and symbol

Portugal has developed a specific culture while being influenced by various civilisations that have crossed the European continent and Mediterranean,[[331]](#cite_note-363) or were introduced when it played an active role during the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Portugal_in_the_Age_of_Discovery "Portugal in the Age of Discovery").[[332]](#cite_note-364) In the 1990s and 2000s (decade), Portugal modernised its public cultural facilities, in addition to the [Calouste Gulbenkian Foundation](/wiki/Calouste_Gulbenkian_Foundation "Calouste Gulbenkian Foundation") established in 1956 in Lisbon.[[333]](#cite_note-365)

These include the [Belém Cultural Centre](/wiki/Bel%C3%A9m_Cultural_Centre "Belém Cultural Centre") in Lisbon,[[334]](#cite_note-366) [Serralves Foundation](/wiki/Serralves_Foundation "Serralves Foundation")[[335]](#cite_note-367) and the [Casa da Música](/wiki/Casa_da_M%C3%BAsica "Casa da Música"),[[336]](#cite_note-368) both in [Porto](/wiki/Porto "Porto"), as well as new public cultural facilities like municipal libraries and concert halls that were built or renovated in many municipalities across the country. Portugal is home to [17](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Portugal "List of World Heritage Sites in Portugal") UNESCO [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), ranking it [9th in Europe and 18th in the world](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites_by_country "World Heritage Sites by country").[[337]](#cite_note-369)

### Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of Portugal](/wiki/Architecture_of_Portugal "Architecture of Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Casa_da_M%C3%BAsica_-_Porto%2C_Portugal%2C_2012_-_001.jpg/250px-Casa_da_M%C3%BAsica_-_Porto%2C_Portugal%2C_2012_-_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Casa_da_M%C3%BAsica_-_Porto,_Portugal,_2012_-_001.jpg)

The [Casa da Música](/wiki/Casa_da_M%C3%BAsica "Casa da Música") in [Porto](/wiki/Porto "Porto"), an example of modern architecture

Traditional architecture is distinctive and include the [Manueline](/wiki/Manueline "Manueline"), also known as Portuguese late [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") a sumptuous, composite Portuguese style of architectural ornamentation of the first decades of the 16th century, followed by the practical and sober [Portuguese Plain](/wiki/Portuguese_Plain_Style_architecture "Portuguese Plain Style architecture") style, and then [Pombaline](/wiki/Pombaline_style "Pombaline style") in the 18th century.[[338]](#cite_note-370)[[339]](#cite_note-371)

A 20th-century interpretation of traditional architecture, [Soft Portuguese style](/wiki/Soft_Portuguese_style "Soft Portuguese style"), appears extensively in major cities, especially Lisbon. Modern Portugal has given the world renowned architects like [Eduardo Souto de Moura](/wiki/Eduardo_Souto_de_Moura "Eduardo Souto de Moura"), [Álvaro Siza Vieira](/wiki/%C3%81lvaro_Siza_Vieira "Álvaro Siza Vieira") (both [Pritzker Prize](/wiki/Pritzker_Prize "Pritzker Prize") winners) and [Gonçalo Byrne](/wiki/Gon%C3%A7alo_Byrne "Gonçalo Byrne"). In Portugal [Tomás Taveira](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1s_Taveira "Tomás Taveira") is also noteworthy, particularly for stadium design.[[340]](#cite_note-372)[[341]](#cite_note-373) The [azulejo](/wiki/Azulejo "Azulejo") is a mainstream, typical element among Portugal's traditional building materials and construction techniques.[[342]](#cite_note-374)[[343]](#cite_note-375)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Portuguese cuisine](/wiki/Portuguese_cuisine "Portuguese cuisine")

See also: [Portuguese wine](/wiki/Portuguese_wine "Portuguese wine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/2012-11-19_at_11-30-37_%288235573655%29.jpg/330px-2012-11-19_at_11-30-37_%288235573655%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2012-11-19_at_11-30-37_(8235573655).jpg)

*[Pastéis de nata](/wiki/Pastel_de_nata "Pastel de nata")* sprinkled with [cinnamon](/wiki/Cinnamon "Cinnamon")

Portuguese cuisine is diverse, part of the [Atlantic diet](/wiki/Atlantic_diet "Atlantic diet"). A significant ingredient in the Portuguese diet is dry [cod](/wiki/Cod "Cod") (*[bacalhau](/wiki/Bacalhau "Bacalhau")* in Portuguese). They say that the Portuguese have mastered 365 different ways to prepare codfish, meaning a cod dish for each day of the year, to name a few [recipes](/wiki/Bacalhau#Bacalhau_dishes "Bacalhau"): [bacalhau à Brás](/wiki/Bacalhau_%C3%A0_Br%C3%A1s "Bacalhau à Brás"), [bacalhau à Gomes de Sá](/wiki/Bacalhau_%C3%A0_Gomes_de_S%C3%A1 "Bacalhau à Gomes de Sá"), [bacalhau com natas](/wiki/Bacalhau_com_natas "Bacalhau com natas"), bacalhau à Minhota, bacalhau espiritual, bacalhau à Lagareiro, bacalhau assado, [bacalhau à Zé do Pipo](/wiki/Bacalhau_%C3%A0_Z%C3%A9_do_Pipo "Bacalhau à Zé do Pipo"), [pastéis de bacalhau](/wiki/Past%C3%A9is_de_bacalhau "Pastéis de bacalhau"). Other fish recipes include grilled [sardines](/wiki/Sardines "Sardines"), [mackerel](/wiki/Mackerel "Mackerel"),[[344]](#cite_note-376) [sea bream](/wiki/Sea_bream "Sea bream"), and [caldeirada](/wiki/Caldeirada "Caldeirada"), a tomato-based [stew](/wiki/Stew "Stew") that can be made from several types of fish or shellfish, with a mix of onion, garlic, bay leaf, potatoes, peppers, parsley.[[345]](#cite_note-377)

Typical Portuguese meat recipes made out of the customary beef, pork, chicken, goat, lamb or duck meat, include *[cozido à portuguesa](/wiki/Cozido_%C3%A0_portuguesa "Cozido à portuguesa")*, *[feijoada](/wiki/Feijoada "Feijoada")*, *[frango de churrasco](/wiki/Frango_de_churrasco "Frango de churrasco")*, *leitão* (roast [suckling pig](/wiki/Suckling_pig "Suckling pig")), *[chanfana](/wiki/Chanfana "Chanfana")* and *[carne de porco à alentejana](/wiki/Carne_de_porco_%C3%A0_alentejana "Carne de porco à alentejana")*. Typical fast food dishes include the [Francesinha](/wiki/Francesinha "Francesinha") (Frenchie) from Porto and *bifanas* (grilled pork) or *prego* (grilled beef) [sandwiches](/wiki/Sandwich "Sandwich").[[346]](#cite_note-378) An egg custard tart pastry, the [pastel de nata](/wiki/Pastel_de_nata "Pastel de nata"), typical and popular among the Portuguese, became popular abroad and among foreign tourists visiting the country as well.[[347]](#cite_note-379)[[348]](#cite_note-380)

Portuguese wines have enjoyed recognition since the Romans, who associated Portugal with their god [Bacchus](/wiki/Dionysus "Dionysus"), due to its climate.[[349]](#cite_note-381) Some of the most well known Portuguese wines are [Vinho Verde](/wiki/Vinho_Verde "Vinho Verde"), [Alvarinho](/wiki/Alvarinho "Alvarinho"), [Vinho do Douro](/wiki/Vinho_do_Douro "Vinho do Douro"), [Vinho do Alentejo](/wiki/Vinho_do_Alentejo "Vinho do Alentejo"), [Vinho do Dão](/wiki/Vinho_do_D%C3%A3o "Vinho do Dão"), [Vinho da Bairrada](/wiki/Vinho_da_Bairrada "Vinho da Bairrada"), [Port Wine](/wiki/Port_Wine "Port Wine"), [Madeira Wine](/wiki/Madeira_Wine "Madeira Wine"), [Moscatel](/wiki/Moscatel "Moscatel") from [Setúbal](/wiki/Set%C3%BAbal "Setúbal") and [Favaios](/wiki/Favaios "Favaios"),[[350]](#cite_note-382) with Portugal being the [10th largest wine producer in the world](/wiki/List_of_wine-producing_regions "List of wine-producing regions").[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOIV202411-383)

### Visual arts

Main article: [Portuguese art](/wiki/Portuguese_art "Portuguese art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/A_Adora%C3%A7%C3%A3o_dos_Magos_%281828%29_-_Domingos_Sequeira.png/250px-A_Adora%C3%A7%C3%A3o_dos_Magos_%281828%29_-_Domingos_Sequeira.png)](/wiki/File:A_Adora%C3%A7%C3%A3o_dos_Magos_(1828)_-_Domingos_Sequeira.png)

*[Adoration of the Magi](/wiki/Adoration_of_the_Magi_(Sequeira) "Adoration of the Magi (Sequeira)")* (1828) by [Domingos Sequeira](/wiki/Domingos_Sequeira "Domingos Sequeira") is an example of a [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism") painting.

Portugal has a [rich history in painting](/wiki/List_of_Portuguese_artists "List of Portuguese artists"). The first well-known painters dating back to the 15th century – like [Nuno Gonçalves](/wiki/Nuno_Gon%C3%A7alves "Nuno Gonçalves") and [Vasco Fernandes](/wiki/Gr%C3%A3o_Vasco "Grão Vasco") – were part of the late Gothic painting period.[[352]](#cite_note-384)[[353]](#cite_note-385) During the Renaissance, Portuguese painting was highly influenced by Northern European painting.[[354]](#cite_note-386) In the Baroque period [Josefa de Óbidos](/wiki/Josefa_de_%C3%93bidos "Josefa de Óbidos") and [Vieira Lusitano](/wiki/Vieira_Lusitano "Vieira Lusitano") were the most prolific painters.[[355]](#cite_note-387)
[José Malhoa](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Malhoa "José Malhoa"), known for his work *Fado*, and [Columbano Bordalo Pinheiro](/wiki/Columbano_Bordalo_Pinheiro "Columbano Bordalo Pinheiro") (who painted the portraits of [Teófilo Braga](/wiki/Te%C3%B3filo_Braga "Teófilo Braga") and [Antero de Quental](/wiki/Antero_de_Quental "Antero de Quental")) were both references in [naturalist painting](/wiki/Naturalist_school_of_painting "Naturalist school of painting").[[356]](#cite_note-388)[[357]](#cite_note-389)

The 20th century saw the arrival of [Modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism"), and along with it came Portuguese painters such as [Amadeo de Souza-Cardoso](/wiki/Amadeo_de_Souza-Cardoso "Amadeo de Souza-Cardoso"), who was heavily influenced by French painters, particularly the Delaunays ([Robert](/wiki/Robert_Delaunay "Robert Delaunay") and [Sonia](/wiki/Sonia_Delaunay "Sonia Delaunay")).[[358]](#cite_note-390) Among his best-known works is *Canção Popular – a Russa e o Fígaro*. Other modernist painters/writers include [Carlos Botelho](/wiki/Carlos_Botelho "Carlos Botelho") and [Almada Negreiros](/wiki/Almada_Negreiros "Almada Negreiros"), friend to the poet [Fernando Pessoa](/wiki/Fernando_Pessoa "Fernando Pessoa"), who painted Pessoa's portrait. He was deeply influenced by both [Cubist](/wiki/Cubist "Cubist") and [Futurist](/wiki/Futurism_(art) "Futurism (art)") trends.[[359]](#cite_note-391)[[360]](#cite_note-392)

Other international figures in visual arts today include painters [Vieira da Silva](/wiki/Vieira_da_Silva "Vieira da Silva"),[[361]](#cite_note-393) [Júlio Pomar](/wiki/J%C3%BAlio_Pomar "Júlio Pomar"),[[362]](#cite_note-394) [Joana Vasconcelos](/wiki/Joana_Vasconcelos "Joana Vasconcelos"),[[363]](#cite_note-395) [Julião Sarmento](/wiki/Juli%C3%A3o_Sarmento "Julião Sarmento")[[364]](#cite_note-396) and [Paula Rego](/wiki/Paula_Rego "Paula Rego").[[365]](#cite_note-397)

### Literature

Main article: [Portuguese literature](/wiki/Portuguese_literature "Portuguese literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Cam%C3%B5es%2C_por_Fern%C3%A3o_Gomes.jpg/250px-Cam%C3%B5es%2C_por_Fern%C3%A3o_Gomes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cam%C3%B5es,_por_Fern%C3%A3o_Gomes.jpg)

[Luís Vaz de Camões](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Vaz_de_Cam%C3%B5es "Luís Vaz de Camões"), legendary poet of the [Portuguese Renaissance](/wiki/Portuguese_Renaissance "Portuguese Renaissance")

Portuguese literature, one of the earliest Western literatures, developed through text as well as song. Until 1350, the [Portuguese-Galician](/wiki/Portuguese-Galician "Portuguese-Galician") [troubadours](/wiki/Troubadour "Troubadour") spread their literary influence to most of the Iberian Peninsula,[[366]](#cite_note-398) like King [D. Dinis](/wiki/Denis_of_Portugal "Denis of Portugal") (1261–1325) who became famous for his poetry. Other kings would write and sponsor works of literature across Portuguese history, like [D. Fernando](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_Portugal "Ferdinand I of Portugal") (1367–1383) who supported Pêro Menino in writing o *Livro da Falcoaria*.[[65]](#cite_note-Carvalho_2014-74)

Adventurer and poet [Luís de Camões](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_de_Cam%C3%B5es "Luís de Camões") (c. 1524–1580) wrote the epic poem *[Os Lusíadas](/wiki/Os_Lus%C3%ADadas "Os Lusíadas")* (*The Lusiads*), with [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil")'s *[Aeneid](/wiki/Aeneid "Aeneid")* as his main influence.[[367]](#cite_note-WDL-399) Modern Portuguese poetry is rooted in neoclassic and contemporary styles, as exemplified by [Bocage](/wiki/Manuel_Maria_Barbosa_du_Bocage "Manuel Maria Barbosa du Bocage") (1765–1805), [Antero de Quental](/wiki/Antero_de_Quental "Antero de Quental") (1842–1891) and [Fernando Pessoa](/wiki/Fernando_Pessoa "Fernando Pessoa") (1888–1935). Modern Portuguese literature is represented by authors such as [Almeida Garrett](/wiki/Almeida_Garrett "Almeida Garrett"), [Camilo Castelo Branco](/wiki/Camilo_Castelo_Branco "Camilo Castelo Branco"), [Eça de Queirós](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Maria_de_E%C3%A7a_de_Queir%C3%B3s "José Maria de Eça de Queirós"), [Fernando Pessoa](/wiki/Fernando_Pessoa "Fernando Pessoa"), [Sophia de Mello Breyner Andresen](/wiki/Sophia_de_Mello_Breyner_Andresen "Sophia de Mello Breyner Andresen"), [António Lobo Antunes](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Lobo_Antunes "António Lobo Antunes"), [Miguel Torga](/wiki/Miguel_Torga "Miguel Torga") and [Agustina Bessa-Luís](/wiki/Agustina_Bessa-Lu%C3%ADs "Agustina Bessa-Luís"). Particularly popular and distinguished is [José Saramago](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Saramago "José Saramago"), recipient of the 1998 [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature").[[368]](#cite_note-400)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Portugal](/wiki/Music_of_Portugal "Music of Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Lu%C3%ADsa_and_Salvador_Sobral%2C_ESC_2017_Winner%27s_press_conference.jpg/500px-Lu%C3%ADsa_and_Salvador_Sobral%2C_ESC_2017_Winner%27s_press_conference.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lu%C3%ADsa_and_Salvador_Sobral,_ESC_2017_Winner%27s_press_conference.jpg)

[Luísa](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADsa_Sobral "Luísa Sobral") and [Salvador Sobral](/wiki/Salvador_Sobral "Salvador Sobral") won the [Eurovision Song Contest 2017](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_2017 "Eurovision Song Contest 2017").

The Music of Portugal encompasses a wide variety of genres. The traditional one is the Portuguese folk music which has deep roots in local customs, utilising instruments such as [bagpipes](/wiki/Bagpipes "Bagpipes") (*[gaita](/wiki/Gaita_(bagpipe) "Gaita (bagpipe)")*),[[369]](#cite_note-401) drums, flutes, tambourines, accordions and ukuleles (*[cavaquinho](/wiki/Cavaquinho "Cavaquinho")*).[[370]](#cite_note-402) Within Portuguese folk music is the renowned genre of [Fado](/wiki/Fado "Fado"), a melancholic urban music that originated in [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon") in the 19th century, probably inside bohemian environments, and is usually associated with the [Portuguese guitar](/wiki/Portuguese_guitar "Portuguese guitar") and *saudade*, or longing.[[371]](#cite_note-403) [Coimbra fado](/wiki/Coimbra_fado "Coimbra fado"), a unique type of "[troubadour](/wiki/Troubadour "Troubadour") serenading" fado, is also noteworthy. Internationally notable performers include [Amália Rodrigues](/wiki/Am%C3%A1lia_Rodrigues "Amália Rodrigues"), [Carlos Paredes](/wiki/Carlos_Paredes "Carlos Paredes"), [José Afonso](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Afonso "José Afonso"), [Mariza](/wiki/Mariza "Mariza"), [Carlos do Carmo](/wiki/Carlos_do_Carmo "Carlos do Carmo"), [António Chainho](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Chainho "António Chainho"), [Mísia](/wiki/M%C3%ADsia "Mísia"), [Dulce Pontes](/wiki/Dulce_Pontes "Dulce Pontes") and [Madredeus](/wiki/Madredeus "Madredeus").[[372]](#cite_note-404)[[373]](#cite_note-405)

Portugal has several summer music festivals, such as *[Festival Sudoeste](/wiki/Festival_Sudoeste "Festival Sudoeste")* in [Zambujeira do Mar](/wiki/Zambujeira_do_Mar "Zambujeira do Mar"),[[374]](#cite_note-406) *[Festival de Paredes de Coura](/wiki/Festival_de_Paredes_de_Coura "Festival de Paredes de Coura")* in [Paredes de Coura](/wiki/Paredes_de_Coura "Paredes de Coura"),[[375]](#cite_note-407) *[Festival Vilar de Mouros](/wiki/Festival_Vilar_de_Mouros "Festival Vilar de Mouros")* near [Caminha](/wiki/Caminha "Caminha"), *[Boom Festival](/wiki/Boom_Festival "Boom Festival")* in [Idanha-a-Nova municipality](/wiki/Idanha-a-Nova "Idanha-a-Nova"), *[NOS Alive](/wiki/NOS_Alive "NOS Alive")*, *Sumol Summer Fest* in [Ericeira](/wiki/Ericeira "Ericeira"), *[Rock in Rio Lisboa](/wiki/Rock_in_Rio#Lisboa,_Portugal,_and_Madrid,_Spain "Rock in Rio")*[[376]](#cite_note-408) and *[Super Bock Super Rock](/wiki/Super_Bock_Super_Rock "Super Bock Super Rock")* in [Greater Lisbon](/wiki/Grande_Lisboa "Grande Lisboa"), plus *[Primavera Sound Porto](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Primavera_Sound_(Portugal) "pt:Primavera Sound (Portugal)")* and *[MEO Marés Vivas](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/MEO_Mar%C3%A9s_Vivas "pt:MEO Marés Vivas")* in the [Greater Porto area](/wiki/Grande_Porto "Grande Porto").[[377]](#cite_note-409)

Portuguese classical music constitutes an important chapter of Western music.[[378]](#cite_note-410) Over the centuries, names of composers and performers have stood out, such as the troubadours [Martim Codax](/wiki/Martim_Codax "Martim Codax")[[379]](#cite_note-411) and [King Denis I](/wiki/Denis_of_Portugal "Denis of Portugal"),[[380]](#cite_note-412) the polyphonists [Duarte Lobo](/wiki/Duarte_Lobo "Duarte Lobo"),[[381]](#cite_note-413) [Filipe de Magalhães](/wiki/Filipe_de_Magalh%C3%A3es "Filipe de Magalhães"), [Manuel Cardoso](/wiki/Manuel_Cardoso_(composer) "Manuel Cardoso (composer)")[[382]](#cite_note-414) and [Pedro de Cristo](/wiki/Pedro_de_Cristo "Pedro de Cristo"), the organist [Manuel Rodrigues Coelho](/wiki/Manuel_Rodrigues_Coelho "Manuel Rodrigues Coelho"), the composer and harpsichordist [Carlos Seixas](/wiki/Carlos_Seixas "Carlos Seixas"),[[383]](#cite_note-415) the singer [Luísa Todi](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADsa_Todi "Luísa Todi"),[[384]](#cite_note-416) symphonist and pianist [João Domingos Bomtempo](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Domingos_Bomtempo "João Domingos Bomtempo")[[385]](#cite_note-417) or composer and musicologist [Fernando Lopes Graça](/wiki/Fernando_Lopes_Gra%C3%A7a "Fernando Lopes Graça").[[386]](#cite_note-418)

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in Portugal](/wiki/Sport_in_Portugal "Sport in Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Cristiano_Ronaldo_Portugal.jpg/250px-Cristiano_Ronaldo_Portugal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cristiano_Ronaldo_Portugal.jpg)

[Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") is widely considered one of the greatest football players of all time.

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is the most popular sport in Portugal. There are several football competitions ranging from local amateur to world-class professional level. All-time greats [Eusébio](/wiki/Eus%C3%A9bio "Eusébio"), [Luís Figo](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Figo "Luís Figo") and [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") are major symbols of [Portuguese football](/wiki/Portuguese_football "Portuguese football") history.[[387]](#cite_note-419)[[388]](#cite_note-420) The [Portugal national football team](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team") has won one [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") title: the [UEFA Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016"), with a 1–0 victory in the [final](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_final "UEFA Euro 2016 final") over [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team"), the tournament hosts.[[389]](#cite_note-421) In addition, Portugal finished first in the [2018–19](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_UEFA_Nations_League "2018–19 UEFA Nations League") and [2024–25 UEFA Nations League](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_UEFA_Nations_League "2024–25 UEFA Nations League"),[[390]](#cite_note-422)[[391]](#cite_note-423) second in the [Euro 2004](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004 "UEFA Euro 2004"),[[392]](#cite_note-424) third in the [1966 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup"),[[393]](#cite_note-425) and fourth in the [2006 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup").[[394]](#cite_note-426)

[Road cycling](/wiki/Road_bicycle_racing "Road bicycle racing"), with [Volta a Portugal](/wiki/Volta_a_Portugal "Volta a Portugal"), is a popular sports event.[[395]](#cite_note-427) In motorsport, Portugal is noted for the [Rally of Portugal](/wiki/Rally_of_Portugal "Rally of Portugal"),[[396]](#cite_note-428) and the [Estoril](/wiki/Estoril_Circuit "Estoril Circuit") and [Algarve Circuits](/wiki/Algarve_Circuit "Algarve Circuit") as well as the revived [Porto Street Circuit](/wiki/Porto_Street_Circuit "Porto Street Circuit") which held a stage of the WTCC for two years.[[397]](#cite_note-429)

In water, Portugal has three major sports: [swimming](/wiki/Swimming_(sport) "Swimming (sport)"), [water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo") and [surfing](/wiki/Surfing "Surfing").[[398]](#cite_note-430) Annually, the country hosts one of the stages of the [World Surf League](/wiki/World_Surf_League "World Surf League") men's and women's *Championship Tour*, the [MEO Rip Curl Pro Portugal](/wiki/MEO_Rip_Curl_Pro_Portugal "MEO Rip Curl Pro Portugal") at the [Supertubos](/wiki/Supertubos "Supertubos") in [Peniche](/wiki/Peniche,_Portugal "Peniche, Portugal").[[399]](#cite_note-431) Northern Portugal has its own original [martial art](/wiki/Martial_art "Martial art"), *[Jogo do Pau](/wiki/Jogo_do_Pau "Jogo do Pau")*, in which fighters use staffs to confront one or several opponents.[[400]](#cite_note-432)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** While Portugal has a *de jure* semi-presidential system, the role of the president is mostly ceremonial, and the country operates under a strong [parliamentary](/wiki/Unitary_parliamentary_republic "Unitary parliamentary republic") leaning.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** The modern day concept of sovereignty or [declaration of independence](/wiki/Declaration_of_independence "Declaration of independence") did not exist at the time, nor was established any notion of its recognition. Portugal was recognised as a kingdom with its own King in [1179](/wiki/Manifestis_Probatum "Manifestis Probatum") by the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") who was the ultimate authority in Europe at the time. The way Europe was seen at the time was as a *[Res publica christiana](/wiki/Res_publica_christiana "Res publica christiana")*.[[4]](#cite_note-5)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-c_7-0)** [Portuguese Constitution adopted in 1976](/wiki/Constitution_of_Portugal "Constitution of Portugal") with several subsequent minor revisions, between 1982 and 2005
4. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** The territory of Portugal includes its land, [internal waters](/wiki/Internal_waters "Internal waters"), and [territorial sea](/wiki/Territorial_waters#Territorial_sea "Territorial waters").[[6]](#cite_note-9)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-18)** The [Escudo](/wiki/Portuguese_escudo "Portuguese escudo") before 2002
6. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** In [mainland Portugal](/wiki/Continental_Portugal "Continental Portugal") and [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira")
7. **[^](#cite_ref-20)** Only in [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores")
8. **[^](#cite_ref-31)** The earliest human trace found in Portugal is the 400,000-year-old [Aroeira 3](/wiki/Aroeira_3 "Aroeira 3"), a *[Homo heidelbergensis](/wiki/Homo_heidelbergensis "Homo heidelbergensis")* skull discovered in the [Cave of Aroeira](/wiki/Cave_of_Aroeira "Cave of Aroeira") in 2014.[[23]](#cite_note-30)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-43)** Ater 929, the [Caliphate of Córdoba](/wiki/Caliphate_of_C%C3%B3rdoba "Caliphate of Córdoba")[[33]](#cite_note-41)[[34]](#cite_note-42)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-79)** Most notably, the Portuguese devised the *[volta do mar](/wiki/Volta_do_mar "Volta do mar")* navigational technique, which enabled safe roundtrip voyages in open sea; this was a major turning point in world history, as it meant that voyagers could now return from long distant places, marking a leap from coastal hugging to deep-sea, long-range navigation, which consequently contributed to further advancements in nautical science and cartography, without that discovery European colonial empires could not have been established.[[69]](#cite_note-78)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-93)** Although some scholars claim that [Portugal may have discovered Australia](/wiki/Theory_of_the_Portuguese_discovery_of_Australia "Theory of the Portuguese discovery of Australia") in 1521,[[78]](#cite_note-88)[[79]](#cite_note-89)[[80]](#cite_note-90) it lacks generally accepted evidence.[[81]](#cite_note-91)[[82]](#cite_note-92)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-98)** Portugal continued to be independent during the [Iberian Union](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union").[[86]](#cite_note-97)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-103)** Most estimates place the number of Portuguese migrants to [Colonial Brazil](/wiki/Colonial_Brazil "Colonial Brazil") during the gold rush of the 18th century at 600,000.[[90]](#cite_note-ibge-102)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-107)** It had an estimated magnitude between 7.7 and 9.0, with casualties ranging from 12,000 to 50,000.[[93]](#cite_note-106)
15. **[^](#cite_ref-126)** These actions were used to affirm Portugal's status as a [transcontinental](/wiki/List_of_transcontinental_countries "List of transcontinental countries") nation and not a colonial empire.[[111]](#cite_note-PTC-125)
16. **[^](#cite_ref-146)** The territory of Portugal includes its land, [internal waters](/wiki/Internal_waters "Internal waters"), and [territorial sea](/wiki/Territorial_waters#Territorial_sea "Territorial waters").[[130]](#cite_note-145)
